narrative:
"And now, having made an end of the preliminaries, we will proceed to the appointment of magistracies."

"Very good."

"In the ordering of a state there are two parts: first, the number of the magistracies, and the mode of establishing them; and, secondly, when they have been established, laws again will have to be provided for each of them, suitable in nature and number. But before electing the magistrates let us stop a little and say a word in season about the election of them."

"What have you got to say?"

"This is what I have to say: every one can see that although the work of legislation is a most important matter, yet if a well-ordered city superadd to good laws unsuitable offices, not only will there be no use in having the good laws -- not only will they be ridiculous and useless, but the greatest political injury and evil will accrue from them."

"Of course."
refashioned:
"Now that we have finished with the preliminaries, let us move on to establishing the offices of government."

"Let's do that."

"When organizing a state, there are two stages: first, deciding how many offices there should be and how they will be filled; and second, once the offices exist, providing each with appropriate laws suited to its function. But before we begin electing officials, let us pause to make an important observation about the election process itself."

"What observation is that?"

"Here is what I want to say: anyone can see that legislation is a matter of the highest importance. But if a well-governed city pairs good laws with poorly chosen officials, those good laws will not merely be wasted -- they will become laughable and pointless, and the greatest political harm will result from them."

"Obviously."
elegant: With preliminaries complete, we turn to establishing magistracies. Two tasks face the state-builder: determining how many offices to create and how to fill them, then equipping each office with proper laws. But before elections begin, one crucial point deserves attention: even the finest laws become useless -- indeed harmful -- when administered by unsuitable officials. Good governance requires the union of good laws and good officers.
summary: The Athenian Stranger transitions from preliminary matters to the appointment of magistrates. He establishes that founding a state requires both creating offices and equipping them with laws, but warns that good laws paired with unsuitable officials will produce not just failure but active political harm.
distilled: Good laws become harmful when administered by unsuitable officials, making the proper selection of magistrates as important as the laws themselves.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). This passage introduces Book 6's central concern: institutional design. The warning that good laws require good administrators parallels Republic 412b-414a on guardian selection -- laws alone are insufficient without virtuous enforcers. Cf. Statesman 293e-294a on the relationship between law and wise rulers. The Athenian's insistence on pausing before elections to discuss method reflects the Platonic commitment to examining foundations before building -- a structural parallel to the Republic's careful construction of the ideal city.

narrative:
"Then now, my friend, let us observe what will happen in the constitution of our intended state. In the first place, you will acknowledge that those who are duly appointed to magisterial power, and their families, should severally have given satisfactory proof of what they are, from youth upward until the time of election; in the next place, those who are to elect should have been trained in habits of law, and be well educated, that they may have a right judgment, and may be able to select or reject men whom they approve or disapprove, as they are worthy of either. But how can we imagine that those who are brought together for the first time, and are strangers to one another, and also uneducated, will avoid making mistakes in the choice of magistrates?"

"Impossible."
refashioned:
"Now then, my friend, let us consider the practical challenge facing our new state. First, you would agree that anyone appointed to hold magisterial power, along with their families, should have demonstrated their character and capability throughout their lives, from youth up to the point of election. Second, the voters themselves must have been raised under good laws and properly educated, so that they can exercise sound judgment in approving or rejecting candidates based on genuine merit. But given these requirements, how can we possibly expect people who have just been brought together for the first time -- strangers to one another and without the benefit of this civic education -- to make wise choices in selecting magistrates?"

"We cannot."
elegant: Two prerequisites exist for sound elections: candidates must have demonstrated their character from youth through a lifetime of public observation, and voters must be educated under good laws to judge merit accurately. A newly assembled colony of strangers, lacking both mutual knowledge and shared civic education, faces an inherent paradox -- the very conditions that make good elections possible do not yet exist.
summary: The Athenian identifies a bootstrapping problem in founding a new state: proper elections require both well-known candidates and educated voters, neither of which exist when colonists are strangers assembled for the first time.
distilled: Good elections require voters who know candidates well and are educated in law, but a new colony has neither, creating a foundational paradox.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). This passage identifies what might be called the 'founding paradox' -- the institutions needed to create good governance presuppose the existence of good governance. This parallels Republic 376c-412a on the education of guardians: virtue in leaders requires prior virtuous formation. The emphasis on family reputation and lifelong scrutiny anticipates the scrutiny (dokimasia) procedures detailed later. Cf. Statesman 308d-311c on the need for political knowledge in selecting rulers.

narrative: "The matter is serious, and excuses will not serve the turn. I will tell you, then, what you and I will have to do, since you, as you tell me, with nine others, have offered to settle the new state on behalf of the people of Crete, and I am to help you by the invention of the present romance. I certainly should not like to leave the tale wandering all over the world without a head -- a headless monster is such a hideous thing."
refashioned: "This is a serious matter, and we cannot simply brush it aside. So let me tell you what you and I must do. You, as you have explained, along with nine others, have volunteered to establish a new colony on behalf of the Cretan people, and my role is to assist you by devising this legislative blueprint. I certainly would not want to leave our plan wandering without proper leadership -- a headless creation is a monstrous thing."
elegant: The founding problem is too serious for excuses. Since Cleinias and his nine colleagues will settle the colony while the Athenian provides its legislative design, their theoretical state must not be left leaderless -- for a headless creation is monstrous.
summary: The Athenian Stranger insists the founding problem cannot be ignored. He acknowledges the division of labor: Cleinias and nine others will physically establish the colony, while the Athenian provides the legislative framework, and this framework must not lack proper leadership.
distilled: The colony's legislative design must include provisions for leadership from the start, since a state without a head is a monstrous thing.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The phrase 'present romance' (mythos) is significant -- the Athenian characterizes the Laws itself as a kind of philosophical fiction, a thought experiment with practical aims. This self-awareness about the dialogic project parallels Republic 376d (the 'noble lie') and Timaeus 26c-e. The 'headless monster' metaphor recalls the organic imagery of Republic 462c-d, where the state is compared to a unified body. The mention of ten founders (Cleinias plus nine) provides concrete grounding for what has been an abstract discussion.

narrative:
"Excellent, Stranger."

"Yes; and I will be as good as my word."

"Let us by all means do as you propose."

"That we will, by the grace of God, if old age will only permit us."

"But God will be gracious."

"Yes; and under his guidance let us consider a further point."

"What is it?"

"Let us remember what a courageously mad and daring creation this our city is."

"What had you in your mind when you said that?"
refashioned:
"Excellent, Stranger."

"Yes, and I intend to follow through on what I have said."

"Then let us proceed exactly as you suggest."

"We shall, God willing, if our old age permits it."

"Surely God will be favorable to us."

"Yes -- and with divine guidance, let us consider one more point."

"What point is that?"

"Let us keep in mind just how boldly ambitious -- even recklessly daring -- our proposed city really is."

"What do you mean by that?"
elegant: The interlocutors affirm their commitment to the project, invoking divine assistance and acknowledging the constraints of old age. The Athenian then urges them to recognize the sheer audacity of what they are attempting -- founding an ideal city is a courageously mad undertaking.
summary: Cleinias and the Athenian affirm their commitment to the legislative project, invoking divine guidance. The Athenian then characterizes their city-building as a boldly daring, even recklessly ambitious, creation.
distilled: The interlocutors commit to their legislative project with divine aid, while the Athenian warns that founding an ideal city is an audaciously daring enterprise.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias (initiating), Athenian Stranger (responding). The invocation of divine guidance is characteristic of the Laws, which takes theology far more seriously than the Republic. The phrase 'courageously mad and daring' (tharraleos manikos kai tolmeros) signals Plato's awareness that practical political founding is more precarious than theoretical construction -- a key distinction between the Laws and Republic. The acknowledgment of old age adds poignancy; these are elderly men designing a state they may not live to see. Cf. Republic 450c-451b on the audacity of philosophical proposals.

narrative:
"I had in my mind the free and easy manner in which we are ordaining that the inexperienced colonists shall receive our laws. Now a man need not be very wise, Cleinias, in order to see that no one can easily receive laws at their first imposition. But if we could anyhow wait until those who have been imbued with them from childhood, and have been nurtured in them, and become habituated to them, take their part in the public elections of the state -- I say, if this could be accomplished, and rightly accomplished by any way or contrivance -- then, I think that there would be very little danger, at the end of the time, of a state thus trained not being permanent."

"A reasonable supposition."
refashioned:
"What I had in mind was this: we are rather casually assuming that inexperienced colonists will simply accept and follow our laws. But it does not take great wisdom to see, Cleinias, that no one readily embraces laws imposed on them for the first time. If only we could somehow wait until people who had been raised with these laws from childhood -- who had absorbed them, been nurtured by them, and grown accustomed to them -- were the ones participating in public elections, then I believe there would be very little risk of the state failing to endure permanently."

"That seems like a reasonable expectation."
elegant: The core difficulty: inexperienced colonists cannot easily accept unfamiliar laws. If only citizens could be raised from childhood within the legal framework, habituated to its ways before participating in governance, the state would be far more likely to endure. Laws take root through lifelong formation, not sudden imposition.
summary: The Athenian identifies a fundamental challenge: laws imposed on inexperienced colonists will not be easily accepted. Genuine stability requires citizens who have been raised within the legal framework from childhood, making habituation the key to a state's permanence.
distilled: Laws cannot simply be imposed on strangers -- they take root only when citizens have been habituated to them from childhood.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). This passage articulates a central theme of the Laws: the priority of habituation (ethos) over mere rational assent in political life. Unlike the Republic's emphasis on philosophical understanding (Republic 514a-521b, the Cave allegory), the Laws stresses that law-abidingness grows through upbringing and custom. This is closer to Aristotle's later ethics (Nicomachean Ethics II.1) and represents a more practically-minded political philosophy. The tension between imposing laws on adults and forming children through laws is the 'bootstrapping problem' of political founding.

narrative:
"Then let us consider if we can find any way out of the difficulty; for I maintain, Cleinias, that the Cnosians, above all the other Cretans, should not be satisfied with barely discharging their duty to the colony, but they ought to take the utmost pains to establish the offices which are first created by them in the best and surest manner. Above all, this applies to the selection of the guardians of the law, who must be chosen first of all, and with the greatest care; the others are of less importance."

"What method can we devise of electing them?"
refashioned:
"Then let us see if we can find a way through this difficulty. I insist, Cleinias, that the Cnosians, more than any other Cretans, should not settle for merely fulfilling their basic obligations to the colony. They must take the greatest possible care to establish the initial offices in the best and most secure way. This applies above all to the selection of the guardians of the law, who must be chosen first and with the most rigorous attention. The other positions are of secondary importance."

"What method should we use to elect them?"
elegant: The Cnosians must go beyond minimal duty to the colony and take supreme care in establishing its first offices. The guardians of the law must be selected first and with the greatest rigor, since they are the most important magistracy. All other offices are secondary to this foundational appointment.
summary: The Athenian urges that the Cnosians take extraordinary care in establishing the colony's first offices, prioritizing above all the selection of guardians of the law as the most critical magistracy.
distilled: The guardians of the law must be chosen first and with the greatest care, as they are the most important office in the new state.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The guardians of the law (nomophylakes) are the Laws' equivalent of the Republic's philosopher-kings, though operating under law rather than above it. Cf. Republic 412b-414a on guardian selection. The emphasis on Cnosus (Knossos) as the mother-city grounds the theoretical discussion in Cretan political reality. The nomophylakes serve as the constitutional anchor -- their quality determines everything that follows, paralleling the Republic's insistence that the character of rulers determines the character of the state (Republic 544d-e).

narrative:
"This will be the method: 'Sons of the Cretans,' I shall say to them, 'inasmuch as the Cnosians have precedence over the other states, they should, in common with those who join this settlement, choose a body of thirty-seven in all, nineteen of them being taken from the settlers, and the remainder from the citizens of Cnosus. Of those latter the Cnosians shall make a present to your colony, and you yourself shall be one of the eighteen, and shall become a citizen of the new state; and if you and they cannot be persuaded to go, the Cnosians may fairly use a little violence in order to make you.'"

"But why, Stranger, do not you and Megillus take a part in our new city?"
refashioned:
"Here is the method I propose. I would address the Cretans as follows: 'People of Crete, since the Cnosians hold precedence among the states, they should join with the new settlers to elect a governing body of thirty-seven members in total -- nineteen chosen from among the settlers and eighteen from the citizens of Cnosus. The Cnosians will contribute these eighteen as a gift to your colony. You yourself, Cleinias, shall be one of the eighteen and become a citizen of the new state. And if you or the others are reluctant to go, the Cnosians would be justified in applying some gentle compulsion to ensure your participation.'"

"But Stranger, why won't you and Megillus join our new city as well?"
elegant: The proposed method: Cnosus and the settlers jointly elect thirty-seven initial magistrates -- nineteen from colonists, eighteen from Cnosians. These Cnosian representatives are a gift to the colony. Cleinias himself shall be among them. If persuasion fails, gentle compulsion is justified to secure the best leadership for the new state.
summary: The Athenian proposes that Cnosus and the settlers jointly choose thirty-seven founding magistrates, with nineteen from the colonists and eighteen from Cnosus, including Cleinias himself. If needed, gentle compulsion may be used to ensure participation.
distilled: Thirty-seven founding magistrates shall be jointly elected from settlers and Cnosians, with Cleinias himself compelled to serve if necessary.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The number thirty-seven is significant -- a prime number that resists factional division. Cleinias's question about why the Athenian and Megillus won't join reveals the dramatic fiction: this is a thought experiment conducted by travelers, not a concrete political plan. The idea that the best citizens may need compulsion to serve echoes Republic 519c-521b, where philosophers must be compelled to return to the cave to govern. The notion of the mother-city 'gifting' leaders to the colony reflects Greek colonial practice.

narrative: "O, Cleinias, Athens is proud, and Sparta too; and they are both a long way off. But you and likewise the other colonists are conveniently situated as you describe. I have been speaking of the way in which the new citizens may be best managed under present circumstances; but in after-ages, if the city continues to exist, let the election be on this wise. All who are horse or foot soldiers, or have seen military service at the proper ages when they were severally fitted for it, shall share in the election of magistrates; and the election shall be held in whatever temple the state deems most venerable, and every one shall carry his vote to the altar of the God, writing down on a tablet the name of the person for whom he votes, and his father's name, and his tribe, and ward; and at the side he shall write his own name in like manner. Any one who pleases may take away any tablet which he does not think properly filled up, and exhibit it in the Agora for a period of not less than thirty days."
refashioned: "Ah, Cleinias, Athens is too proud for that, and Sparta likewise -- and both are far away. But you and the other colonists are well positioned for this undertaking. I have been describing how the first citizens might best be organized under the present circumstances. But in future generations, should the city endure, elections should proceed as follows: all who have served as cavalry or infantry, or who performed military service at the appropriate age, shall have the right to vote for magistrates. Elections shall be held in whichever temple the state considers most sacred. Each voter shall carry his ballot to the altar of the god, writing on a tablet the name of his chosen candidate along with the candidate's father's name, tribe, and ward, and beside these his own identifying information in the same format. Anyone may challenge a ballot that appears improperly completed by removing it and displaying it publicly in the Agora for a period of no fewer than thirty days."
elegant: Athens and Sparta are too proud and too distant to participate. For the colony's future, the permanent election procedure shall be: all citizens with military service may vote at the most venerable temple, writing their candidate's name, lineage, tribe, and ward on tablets deposited at the altar, along with their own identity. Any improperly completed ballot may be publicly challenged in the Agora for thirty days.
summary: The Athenian explains that Athens and Sparta are too distant to contribute leaders. He then establishes the permanent election procedure: military veterans vote by inscribing tablets at a sacred temple, with a thirty-day public challenge period for improperly completed ballots.
distilled: Future elections shall use written ballots deposited at a temple altar by military veterans, with a thirty-day public review period for challenges.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. Athens and Sparta's exclusion is both practical and symbolic -- the Laws' political model is deliberately distinct from both Athenian democracy and Spartan oligarchy. The franchise restricted to military veterans echoes the connection between civic duty and political rights found across Greek political thought. The sacred setting of elections (at temple altars) infuses political participation with religious solemnity, a hallmark of the Laws' integration of politics and theology. The public challenge mechanism (displaying questionable ballots in the Agora) creates transparency. Cf. Republic 557a-558c on democratic participation.

narrative:
"The tablets which are judged to be first, to the number of 300, shall be shown by the magistrates to the whole city, and the citizens shall in like manner select from these the candidates whom they prefer; and this second selection, to the number of 100, shall be again exhibited to the citizens; in the third, let any one who pleases select whom he pleases out of the 100, walking through the parts of victims, and let them choose for magistrates and proclaim the seven and thirty who have the greatest number of votes. But who, Cleinias and Megillus, will order for us in the colony all this matter of the magistrates, and the scrutinies of them? If we reflect, we shall see that cities which are in process of construction like ours must have some such persons, who cannot possibly be elected before there are any magistrates; and yet they must be elected in some way, and they are not to be inferior men, but the best possible. For as the proverb says, 'a good beginning is half the business'; and 'to have begun well' is praised by all, and in my opinion is a great deal more than half the business, and has never been praised by any one enough."

"That is very true."

"Then let us recognize the difficulty, and make clear to our own minds how the beginning is to be accomplished. There is only one proposal which I have to offer, and that is one which, under our circumstances, is both necessary and expedient."

"What is it?"
refashioned:
"The top 300 vote-getters shall be publicly displayed by the magistrates for the whole city to see, and the citizens shall then select their preferred candidates from among them. This second round of selection, narrowing the field to 100, shall again be exhibited publicly. In the third round, any citizen who wishes may choose from the 100 by walking through sacrificial offerings, and the thirty-seven who receive the most votes shall be proclaimed as magistrates.

But here is the critical question, Cleinias and Megillus: who will organize all of this -- the elections and the scrutinies of candidates -- in a colony that does not yet exist? When we think about it, we see that a city under construction needs organizers who cannot be elected through the very process that does not yet exist. And yet they must be chosen somehow, and they must not be mediocre men but the best available. As the proverb says, 'A good beginning is half the work.' And in my view, 'to have begun well' is worth far more than half -- it has never been praised enough."

"That is absolutely true."

"Then let us acknowledge this difficulty squarely and think clearly about how to accomplish this beginning. I have only one proposal to offer, and it is one that our circumstances make both necessary and practical."

"What is it?"
elegant: The election process narrows candidates through three rounds: 300 to 100 to the final 37 magistrates. But a deeper problem remains: who organizes the first elections in a city that has no magistrates yet? The best possible people must somehow be chosen before the electoral machinery exists. A good beginning is more than half the work -- it is the indispensable foundation.
summary: The Athenian describes a three-stage election filtering 300 candidates to 100 to 37 final magistrates. He then confronts the bootstrapping problem: someone must organize the first elections before any magistrates exist, and these organizers must be the best possible people. A good beginning, he insists, is more than half the work.
distilled: Elections narrow candidates through three rounds to thirty-seven magistrates, but the founding paradox remains: the best people must organize elections before any electoral system exists.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The three-stage winnowing process (300 to 100 to 37) combines democratic breadth with meritocratic selection -- a characteristic 'mixed constitution' approach that distinguishes the Laws from both pure democracy and the Republic's philosopher-kingship. The proverb 'a good beginning is half the business' (arche hemisy pantos) is attributed to various Greek sources. The 'walking through parts of victims' refers to a solemn sacrificial oath-taking ceremony. The founding paradox -- needing excellent organizers before any selection mechanism exists -- drives the solution in the next passage.

narrative:
"I maintain that this colony of ours has a father and mother, who are no other than the colonizing state. Well I know that many colonies have been, and will be, at enmity with their parents. But in early days the child, as in a family, loves and is beloved; even if there come a time later when the tie is broken, still, while he is in want of education, he naturally loves his parents and is beloved by them, and flies to his relatives for protection, and finds in them his only natural allies in time of need; and this parental feeling already exists in the Cnosians, as is shown by their care of the new city; and there is a similar feeling on the part of the young city towards Cnosus.

And I repeat what I was saying -- for there is no harm in repeating a good thing -- that the Cnosians should take a common interest in all these matters, and choose, as far as they can, the eldest and best of the colonists, to the number of not less than a hundred; and let there be another hundred of the Cnosians themselves. These, I say, on their arrival, should have a joint care that the magistrates should be appointed according to law, and that when they are appointed they should undergo a scrutiny. When this has been effected, the Cnosians shall return home, and the new city do the best she can for her own preservation and happiness.

I would have the seven-and-thirty now, and in all future time, chosen to fulfil the following duties: Let them, in the first place, be the guardians of the law; and, secondly, of the registers in which each one registers before the magistrate the amount of his property, excepting four minae which are allowed to citizens of the first class, three allowed to the second, two to the third, and a single mina to the fourth."
refashioned:
"I maintain that our colony has a father and mother -- namely, the founding state itself. I am well aware that many colonies have come into conflict with their parent cities, and this will continue to happen. But in its early days, a colony is like a child in a family: it loves and is loved. Even if the bond eventually breaks, while the child still needs guidance, there is a natural affection between parent and offspring, and the child turns to its family for protection as its only natural ally. This parental feeling already exists among the Cnosians, demonstrated by their care for the new settlement, and the young city reciprocates that feeling toward Cnosus.

Let me repeat what I said before -- for a good point bears repeating. The Cnosians should take an active interest in all these founding matters and select the oldest and best colonists, at least one hundred of them, along with another hundred from among the Cnosians themselves. Together, these two hundred should jointly oversee the appointment of magistrates according to law and ensure that each appointee undergoes proper scrutiny. Once this is accomplished, the Cnosians shall return home, and the new city must manage its own preservation and prosperity as best it can.

The thirty-seven magistrates, both now and in all future times, shall fulfill these duties: first, they shall serve as guardians of the law; and second, they shall oversee the property registers in which every citizen declares his wealth before the magistrate, with exemptions of four minae for the first property class, three for the second, two for the third, and one mina for the fourth."
elegant: The colony relates to its founding state as child to parent -- bound by natural affection and dependent on guidance during its formative period. To solve the founding paradox, two hundred elders -- one hundred colonists and one hundred Cnosians -- shall jointly oversee the first magistrate appointments and their scrutiny, after which the Cnosians return home. The thirty-seven permanent magistrates shall guard the laws and oversee property registration, with graduated exemptions by class.
summary: The Athenian solves the bootstrapping problem through the parent-colony metaphor: two hundred elders from both Cnosus and the settlers will jointly establish the first magistrates. The thirty-seven guardians of the law shall permanently oversee legislation and property registration with class-based exemptions.
distilled: The founding paradox is resolved by having two hundred elders from the mother-city and colony jointly establish the first magistrates, who then serve as permanent guardians of law and property registration.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The parent-colony metaphor draws on actual Greek colonial practice (apoikia), where mother-cities maintained religious and cultural ties with their colonies. The solution -- importing experienced leaders temporarily -- is pragmatic rather than philosophical, distinguishing the Laws from the Republic's more idealized approach. The thirty-seven guardians (nomophylakes) combine legislative oversight with economic regulation through property registration. The graduated property exemptions establish a four-class census system reminiscent of Solon's reforms at Athens. Cf. Republic 415a-417b on guardian property arrangements, which are far more radical (communal property). The Laws' approach is more moderate and realistic.

narrative:
"And if any one, despising the laws for the sake of gain, be found to possess anything more which has not been registered, let all that he has in excess be confiscated, and let him be liable to a suit which shall be the reverse of honourable or fortunate. And let any one who will, indict him on the charge of loving base gains, and proceed against him before the guardians of the law. And if he be cast, let him lose his share of the public possessions, and when there is any public distribution, let him have nothing but his original lot; and let him be written down a condemned man as long as he lives, in some place in which any one who pleases can read about his offences.

The guardian of the law shall not hold office longer than twenty years, and shall not be less than fifty years of age when he is elected; or if he is elected when he is sixty years of age, he shall hold office for ten years only; and upon the same principle, he must not imagine that he will be permitted to hold such an important office as that of guardian of the laws after he is seventy years of age, if he live so long."
refashioned:
"If anyone is found to have concealed property out of contempt for the law and desire for gain, all undeclared assets shall be confiscated, and he shall face a lawsuit that will bring him nothing but disgrace. Any citizen may bring a charge of base greed against him before the guardians of the law. If convicted, he shall lose his share of public assets, and whenever public distributions occur, he shall receive nothing beyond his original allotment. His name shall be publicly recorded as a condemned man for the rest of his life, inscribed in a place where anyone can read about his offenses.

Regarding the guardians of the law themselves: no guardian shall serve longer than twenty years, and none shall be elected before the age of fifty. If elected at sixty, he shall serve only ten years. On this same principle, no one shall hold this critical office past the age of seventy, however long he may live."
elegant: Anyone who conceals property to evade registration shall have the excess confiscated, face public prosecution for base greed, lose his share of public goods, and be permanently recorded as a condemned man. The guardians of the law themselves must be at least fifty, serve no more than twenty years, and retire by seventy -- ensuring both maturity and renewal in the most important office.
summary: Citizens who hide property face confiscation, prosecution, and permanent public disgrace. The guardians of the law must be at least fifty years old when elected, serve no longer than twenty years, and must not hold office past seventy.
distilled: Hidden property is confiscated and the offender permanently disgraced, while guardians of the law serve from age fifty to a maximum of seventy.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The severe penalties for property concealment -- confiscation, exclusion from public distributions, permanent public shaming -- reflect the Laws' deep concern with economic equality and transparency. Unlike the Republic's radical communism for guardians (Republic 416d-417b), the Laws permits private property but regulates it strictly. The age requirements for nomophylakes (50-70) emphasize the value of mature judgment. The twenty-year term limit prevents entrenchment while ensuring stability. Cf. Republic 412c on selecting guardians who are 'elderly' and have been tested over time. The public inscription of offenses is a form of social enforcement through shame, a characteristically Greek mechanism.

narrative:
"These are the three first ordinances about the guardians of the law; as the work of legislation progresses, each law in turn will assign to them their further duties. And now we may proceed in order to speak of the election of other officers; for generals have to be elected, and these again must have their ministers, commanders, and colonels of horse, and commanders of brigades of foot, who would be more rightly called by their popular name of brigadiers.

The guardians of the law shall propose as generals men who are natives of the city, and a selection from the candidates proposed shall be made by those who are or have been of the age for military service."
refashioned:
"These are the first three ordinances concerning the guardians of the law. As legislation proceeds, additional duties will be assigned to them by each successive law. Now let us move on to the election of other officials. Generals must be elected, and they in turn need subordinate officers: commanders, cavalry colonels, and leaders of infantry brigades -- the latter more properly called by their common name, brigadiers.

The guardians of the law shall nominate candidates for general, choosing from native citizens of the city. The final selection from among these nominees shall be made by all citizens who are currently serving or have previously served in the military at the appropriate age."
elegant: Three founding ordinances govern the guardians of the law, with more duties to follow. Next come the military offices: generals, cavalry commanders, and brigadiers. The guardians of the law nominate general candidates from native citizens, and current or former soldiers make the final selection through voting.
summary: Having established three founding rules for the guardians of the law, the Athenian turns to military offices. The guardians nominate general candidates from native citizens, and military veterans make the final selection.
distilled: The guardians of the law nominate general candidates, and military veterans elect from among the nominees.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The military command structure -- generals (strategoi), cavalry colonels (hipparchoi), brigadiers (taxiarchoi) -- mirrors historical Athenian and Greek military organization. The nomination by guardians followed by election by veterans creates a two-stage meritocratic process: expertise in nominating, broad participation in choosing. This balances elite judgment with popular consent, characteristic of the Laws' mixed constitution. Cf. Republic 525b on the philosopher-king's need for military knowledge, and Statesman 304e-305a on the relationship between military command and political authority.

narrative:
"And if one who is not proposed is thought by somebody to be better than one who is, let him name whom he prefers in the place of whom, and make oath that he is better, and propose him; and whichever of them is approved by vote shall be admitted to the final selection; and the three who have the greatest number of votes shall be appointed generals, and superintendents of military affairs, after previously undergoing a scrutiny, like the guardians of the law.

And let the generals thus elected propose twelve brigadiers, one for each tribe; and there shall be a right of counterproposal as in the case of the generals, and the voting and decision shall take place in the same way. Until the prytanes and council are elected, the guardians of the law shall convene the assembly in some holy spot which is suitable to the purpose, placing the hoplites by themselves, and the cavalry by themselves, and in a third division all the rest of the army. All are to vote for the generals and for the colonels of horse, but the brigadiers are to be voted for only by those who carry shields -- that is, the hoplites.

Let the body of cavalry choose phylarchs for the generals; but captains of light troops, or archers, or any other division of the army, shall be appointed by the generals for themselves. There only remains the appointment of officers of cavalry: these shall be proposed by the same persons who proposed the generals, and the election and the counter-proposal of other candidates shall be arranged in the same way as in the case of the generals, and let the cavalry vote and the infantry look on at the election; the two who have the greatest number of votes shall be the leaders of all the horse. Disputes about the voting may be raised once or twice; but if the dispute be raised a third time, the officers who preside at the several elections shall decide."
refashioned:
"If anyone believes that a person not on the official nominee list is better than one who is, he may name his preferred candidate in place of the other, swear under oath that his candidate is superior, and formally propose him. Whichever candidate is then approved by vote enters the final selection. The three who receive the most votes shall be appointed generals and superintendents of military affairs, after first undergoing a scrutiny like the guardians of the law.

The elected generals shall then propose twelve brigadiers, one per tribe. The same right of counterproposal that applies to generals shall apply here, with voting and decisions following the same procedure. Until the prytanes and council have been elected, the guardians of the law shall convene the assembly at a suitable sacred location, with hoplites grouped separately from cavalry, and all remaining forces in a third division. Everyone votes for generals and cavalry colonels, but only shield-bearing hoplites vote for brigadiers.

The cavalry shall choose tribal commanders for the generals. Captains of light troops, archers, and other specialized divisions shall be appointed directly by the generals. For the two cavalry commanders, nominations shall follow the same process as for generals, with the same right of counterproposal. The cavalry votes while the infantry observes; the two candidates with the most votes lead all the cavalry. Disputed results may be challenged once or twice, but if raised a third time, the presiding election officers shall make the final decision."
elegant: Any citizen may counter-nominate candidates under oath. Three generals are elected by highest vote after scrutiny. Generals nominate twelve tribal brigadiers using the same counterproposal process. The assembly is organized by military branch: all vote for generals and cavalry colonels; hoplites alone vote for brigadiers; cavalry alone vote for cavalry commanders. Light troop captains are appointed directly by generals. Voting disputes may be raised twice but are resolved definitively on the third challenge by presiding officers.
summary: The election process allows sworn counter-nominations. Three generals are elected by majority vote with scrutiny. Twelve brigadiers are nominated by generals with the same counterproposal rights. Voting is organized by military branch, and election disputes are settled definitively after two challenges.
distilled: Military elections proceed through nomination, counter-nomination under oath, and branch-specific voting, with disputes settled definitively by presiding officers after two challenges.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage reveals the Laws' sophisticated electoral design: the counterproposal mechanism (anticheirotonia) prevents nomination monopolies by allowing any citizen to challenge official nominees under oath. The branch-specific voting -- hoplites vote for brigadiers, cavalry for cavalry commanders -- ensures that those with relevant expertise choose their own leaders. The three-challenge limit on disputes prevents endless litigation. This mixed system combines democratic participation (broad voting rights), meritocratic filtering (nomination and scrutiny), and practical specialization (branch-specific elections). Cf. Republic 374d-376c on matching roles to natures.

narrative:
"The council shall consist of 30 x 12 members -- 360 will be a convenient number for sub-division. If we divide the whole number into four parts of ninety each, we get ninety counsellors for each class.

First, all the citizens shall select candidates from the first class; they shall be compelled to vote, and, if they do not, shall be duly fined. When the candidates have been selected, some one shall mark them down; this shall be the business of the first day. And on the following day, candidates shall be selected from the second class in the same manner and under the same conditions as on the previous day; and on the third day a selection shall be made from the third class, at which every one may, if he likes, vote, and the three first classes shall be compelled to vote; but the fourth and lowest class shall be under no compulsion, and any member of this class who does not vote shall not be punished.

On the fourth day candidates shall be selected from the fourth and smallest class; they shall be selected by all, but he who is of the fourth class shall suffer no penalty, nor he who is of the third, if he be not willing to vote; but he who is of the first or second class, if he does not vote shall be punished -- he who is of the second class shall pay a fine of triple the amount which was exacted at first, and he who is of the first class quadruple.

On the fifth day the rulers shall bring out the names noted down, for all the citizens to see, and every man shall choose out of them, under pain, if he do not, of suffering the first penalty; and when they have chosen out of each of the classes, they shall choose one-half of them by lot, who shall undergo a scrutiny -- these are to form the council for the year."
refashioned:
"The council shall have 360 members -- 30 times 12 -- a number conveniently divisible. Divided into four parts of ninety, this gives ninety councillors from each property class.

On the first day, all citizens shall select candidates from the first class. Voting is compulsory, with fines for non-compliance. The selected names shall be recorded. On the second day, the same process applies to the second class under identical conditions. On the third day, candidates from the third class are selected: voting is mandatory for the first three classes but optional for the fourth, with no penalty for abstention.

On the fourth day, candidates from the fourth and smallest class are chosen. Everyone may vote, but members of the fourth and third classes face no penalty for abstaining. However, members of the second class who fail to vote pay a fine three times the original amount, and first-class members pay four times.

On the fifth day, all recorded names are publicly displayed. Every citizen must choose from among them or face the original fine. From each class, half the selected candidates are then chosen by lot and subjected to scrutiny. These shall form the council for the year."
elegant: A 360-member council, ninety from each of four property classes, is elected over five days. Voting obligations increase with wealth: the richest classes face escalating fines for non-participation, while the poorest vote voluntarily. After five days of selection, half the candidates from each class are chosen by lot and scrutinized to form the annual council. This graduated compulsion ensures the wealthy cannot abdicate civic responsibility while respecting the constraints of the poor.
summary: The council of 360 is elected over five days, ninety members from each of four property classes. Compulsory voting requirements increase with wealth class, with escalating fines for non-participation. Final selection combines vote with lot and scrutiny.
distilled: A 360-member council is elected over five days with graduated compulsory voting, combining popular selection, lottery, and scrutiny across four property classes.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The council (boule) of 360 is deliberately designed for mathematical convenience (divisible by 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, etc.). The graduated compulsion -- wealthier classes face heavier fines for non-voting -- is a sophisticated mechanism for ensuring oligarchic responsibility without excluding democratic participation. This anticipates the 'two equalities' discussion in 6.15-16. The combination of election, lot, and scrutiny blends democratic (lot), oligarchic (class-based), and meritocratic (scrutiny) elements. Cf. Republic 546a-547c on the degeneration of constitutions when class balance is disrupted. The five-day procedure is more elaborate than any historical Greek electoral system.

narrative:
"The mode of election which has been described is in a mean between monarchy and democracy, and such a mean the state ought always to observe; for servants and masters never can be friends, nor good and bad, merely because they are declared to have equal privileges. For to unequals equals become unequal, if they are not harmonized by measure; and both by reason of equality, and by reason of inequality, cities are filled with seditions.

The old saying, that 'equality makes friendship,' is happy and also true; but there is obscurity and confusion as to what sort of equality is meant. For there are two equalities which are called by the same name, but are in reality in many ways almost the opposite of one another; one of them may be introduced without difficulty, by any state or any legislator in the distribution of honours: this is the rule of measure, weight, and number, which regulates and apportions them. But there is another equality, of a better and higher kind, which is not so easily recognized. This is the judgment of Zeus; among men it avails but little; that little, however, is the source of the greatest good to individuals and states."
refashioned:
"The electoral system I have described strikes a mean between monarchy and democracy, and the state should always maintain this balance. Servants and masters can never truly be friends, nor can the virtuous and the base, simply by being declared equal in privilege. When unequals are treated as equals without proper calibration, the equality itself becomes a source of inequality. Cities are torn apart by sedition both when there is too much equality and when there is too much inequality.

The ancient maxim that 'equality creates friendship' is both well-put and true, but there is real confusion about what kind of equality is meant. There are actually two types of equality that share the same name but are, in practice, nearly opposite. The first is simple arithmetic equality -- the rule of measure, weight, and number -- which any state or legislator can easily apply when distributing honors. The second is a higher and better form of equality, far harder to recognize. This is the judgment of Zeus himself. Among human beings it operates only to a small degree, but that small degree is the source of the greatest good for both individuals and states."
elegant: The ideal electoral system occupies a mean between monarchy and democracy. Treating unequals as equals produces not fairness but sedition; both excessive equality and excessive inequality destroy cities. Two kinds of equality exist: arithmetic equality (measure, weight, number), which is easy to apply, and proportional equality -- the judgment of Zeus -- which gives to each according to their nature. This higher equality is rare among humans but is the source of the greatest political good.
summary: The Athenian explains that the electoral system should balance monarchy and democracy. He distinguishes two types of equality: simple arithmetic equality and a higher proportional equality -- the judgment of Zeus -- which distributes according to merit and nature, and which, though rare, is the source of the greatest good.
distilled: True political equality is not arithmetic sameness but proportional justice -- giving to each according to their nature and merit -- a rare but supreme principle.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This is one of the most philosophically significant passages in Book 6. The distinction between arithmetic equality (to ison) and geometric/proportional equality (to analogon) is central to Greek political philosophy. Cf. Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics V.3-4 and Politics III.9 on distributive justice. The 'judgment of Zeus' (Dios krisis) elevates proportional equality to a divine principle. This directly parallels Republic 558c on democracy's indiscriminate equality and Gorgias 508a on geometric equality as the ordering principle of cosmos and polis. The 'mean between monarchy and democracy' formulation anticipates Aristotle's mixed constitution (Politics IV.8-9).

narrative:
"For it gives to the greater more, and to the inferior less and in proportion to the nature of each; and, above all, greater honour always to the greater virtue, and to the less less; and to either in proportion to their respective measure of virtue and education. And this is justice, and is ever the true principle of states, at which we ought to aim, and according to this rule order the new city which is now being founded, and any other city which may be hereafter founded.

To this the legislator should look -- not to the interests of tyrants one or more, or to the power of the people, but to justice always; which, as I was saying, the distribution of natural equality among unequals in each case. But there are times at which every state is compelled to use the words, 'just,' 'equal,' in a secondary sense, in the hope of escaping in some degree from factions. For equity and indulgence are infractions of the perfect and strict rule of justice. And this is the reason why we are obliged to use the equality of the lot, in order to avoid the discontent of the people; and so we invoke God and fortune in our prayers, and beg that they themselves will direct the lot with a view to supreme justice.

And therefore, although we are compelled to use both equalities, we should use that into which the element of chance enters as seldom as possible."
refashioned:
"This higher equality gives more to the greater and less to the lesser, always in proportion to each person's nature. Above all, it assigns greater honor to greater virtue and lesser honor to lesser virtue, each receiving in proportion to their measure of excellence and education. This is justice -- the true and eternal principle of political organization. It is this principle that should guide the ordering of our new city and any city founded in the future.

The legislator should aim at this principle of justice -- not at the interests of one or several tyrants, nor at the power of the masses, but at justice itself, which means distributing natural equality among unequals appropriately in each case. Yet there are times when every state must use words like 'just' and 'equal' in a weaker, secondary sense, hoping thereby to reduce factional conflict. Equity and indulgence represent departures from the strict rule of perfect justice. This is why we are sometimes forced to use the lottery -- to prevent popular discontent -- and we pray that God and fortune will guide the lot toward supreme justice.

Therefore, while we must use both forms of equality, we should employ the one involving chance as rarely as possible."
elegant: Proportional equality assigns honor according to virtue and education -- this is justice itself, the true principle of political organization. The legislator must serve neither tyrants nor mobs but justice alone. Yet practical governance sometimes requires arithmetic equality and the lottery to prevent faction. We pray that chance serves justice, but we should minimize its use, preferring merit-based distribution wherever possible.
summary: Proportional equality -- distributing honor according to virtue -- is the true principle of justice and should guide all political organization. Yet states sometimes must use arithmetic equality and lottery to prevent faction, though these departures from perfect justice should be minimized.
distilled: Justice means proportional distribution according to virtue, but practical governance sometimes requires the compromise of lottery and arithmetic equality to prevent civil strife.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage completes the two-equalities doctrine begun in 6.15. The proportional principle -- 'greater honor to greater virtue' -- is the Laws' version of the Republic's justice principle (Republic 433a-b: each doing what they are suited for). The pragmatic concession to lottery reflects the Laws' realism compared to the Republic. The legislator's duty to serve 'justice always' rather than any faction echoes Republic 342d-e (rulers should serve the ruled). The prayer that God will direct the lot reconciles divine providence with political compromise -- a theological move absent from the Republic but central to the Laws.

narrative:
"Thus, O my friends, and for the reasons given, should a state act which would endure and be saved. But as a ship sailing on the sea has to be watched night and day, in like manner a city also is sailing on a sea of politics, and is liable to all sorts of insidious assaults; and therefore from morning to night, and from night to morning, rulers must join hands with rulers, and watchers with watchers, receiving and giving up their trust in a perpetual succession.

Now a multitude can never fulfil a duty of this sort with anything like energy. Moreover, the greater number of the senators will have to be left during the greater part of the year to order their concerns at their own homes. They will therefore have to be arranged in twelve portions, answering to the twelve months, and furnish guardians of the state, each portion for a single month.

Their business is to be at hand and receive any foreigner or citizen who comes to them, whether to give information, or to put one of those questions, to which, when asked by other cities, a city should give an answer, and to which, if she ask them herself, she should receive an answer; or again, when there is a likelihood of internal commotions, which are always liable to happen in some form or other, they will, if they can, prevent their occurring; or if they have already occurred, will lose no time in making them known to the city, and healing the evil.

Wherefore, also, this which is the presiding body of the state ought always to have the control of their assemblies, and of the dissolutions of them, ordinary as well as extraordinary. All this is to be ordered by the twelfth part of the council, which is always to keep watch together with the other officers of the state during one portion of the year, and to rest during the remaining eleven portions."
refashioned:
"For the reasons I have given, this is how a state must act if it hopes to endure. Just as a ship at sea must be watched night and day, so a city sails on a sea of politics and is vulnerable to all manner of threats. Therefore, from morning to night and from night to morning, rulers must relay authority to rulers, watchers to watchers, in unbroken succession.

Now, a large body can never maintain this kind of vigilance with real energy. Moreover, most senators will need to spend the greater part of the year managing their own affairs at home. The council should therefore be divided into twelve groups corresponding to the twelve months, with each group serving as guardians of the state for one month.

Their responsibilities during their month of duty include: receiving any foreigner or citizen who comes with information or questions, particularly the kind of diplomatic inquiries that cities regularly exchange; watching for signs of internal unrest, which is always liable to arise in some form, and preventing such disturbances if possible, or, if they have already broken out, reporting them immediately to the city and addressing the problem without delay.

This presiding body should therefore control the convening and dissolving of all assemblies, both regular and emergency sessions. Everything shall be managed by whichever twelfth of the council is on duty, keeping watch alongside the other officers of state during their month, and resting for the remaining eleven."
elegant: A city sails on a sea of politics, requiring constant vigilance like a ship at sea. Since a large council cannot maintain perpetual watchfulness, the 360-member body is divided into twelve monthly rotations of thirty. Each month's group handles diplomacy, receives petitions, prevents or responds to internal unrest, and controls the assembly schedule. Governance becomes a relay of continuous, shared responsibility.
summary: The Athenian compares the state to a ship requiring constant watch. The council of 360 is divided into twelve monthly rotations, each group handling diplomacy, responding to emergencies, and controlling assembly schedules during their month of duty.
distilled: The council is divided into twelve monthly rotations for perpetual vigilance, since a city, like a ship at sea, requires constant watching.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The ship-of-state metaphor is one of Plato's most enduring images, developed extensively in Republic 488a-489a (the 'ship of fools'). Here it serves a practical rather than polemical purpose -- justifying rotational governance. The twelve-month council rotation resembles the Athenian prytany system, where each of the ten tribes' contingent served as prytaneis for about 36 days. The Laws' twelve-fold division aligns with its pervasive use of the number 12 (tribes, months, land divisions). The 'sea of politics' metaphor emphasizes that governance is an endless, dangerous voyage requiring unbroken attention.

narrative:
"Thus will the city be fairly ordered. And now, who is to have the superintendence of the country, and what shall be the arrangement? Seeing that the whole city and the entire country have been both of them divided into twelve portions, ought there not to be appointed superintendents of the streets of the city, and of the houses, and buildings, and harbours, and the agora, and fountains, and sacred domains, and temples, and the like?"

"To be sure there ought."
refashioned:
"With this arrangement the city will be properly organized. Now, who shall have oversight of the surrounding territory, and how should this be structured? Since both the city and the countryside have been divided into twelve portions, should we not appoint officials to supervise the city's streets, houses, buildings, harbors, marketplace, fountains, sacred precincts, temples, and everything of that nature?"

"We certainly should."
elegant: With the council organized, attention turns to territorial administration. Since both city and country are divided into twelve portions, superintendents must be appointed for streets, buildings, harbors, the marketplace, fountains, sacred precincts, and temples.
summary: The Athenian transitions from council organization to territorial administration, proposing that superintendents be appointed for all aspects of urban infrastructure -- streets, buildings, harbors, the agora, fountains, and temples -- following the twelve-fold division.
distilled: Superintendents must be appointed for every aspect of urban infrastructure, following the twelve-fold division of city and territory.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The comprehensive list of urban infrastructure -- streets, houses, buildings, harbors, agora, fountains, sacred domains, temples -- reveals the Laws' detailed administrative vision. Unlike the Republic, which focuses on the soul of the city, the Laws attends to its body: physical infrastructure requires institutional oversight. The twelve-fold division unifies territorial and political organization. This passage transitions from constitutional architecture (offices, elections, council) to administrative governance (daily management of the city), reflecting the Laws' practical orientation.

narrative:
"Let us assume, then, that there ought to be servants of the temples, and priests and priestesses. There must also be superintendents of roads and buildings, who will have a care of men, that they may do no harm, and also of beasts, both within the enclosure and in the suburbs. Three kinds of officers will thus have to be appointed, in order that the city may be suitably provided according to her needs.

Those who have the care of the city shall be called wardens of the city; and those who have the care of the agora shall be called wardens of the agora; and those who have the care of the temples shall be called priests. Those who hold hereditary offices as priests or priestesses, shall not be disturbed; but if there be few or none such, as is probable at the foundation of a new city, priests and priestesses shall be appointed to be servants of the Gods who have no servants.

Some of our officers shall be elected, and others appointed by lot, those who are of the people and those who are not of the people mingling in a friendly manner in every place and city, that the state may be as far as possible of one mind.

The officers of the temples shall be appointed by lot; in this way their election will be committed to God, that he may do what is agreeable to him. And he who obtains a lot shall undergo a scrutiny, first, as to whether he is sound of body and of legitimate birth; and in the second place, in order to show that he is of a perfectly pure family, not stained with homicide or any similar impiety in his own person, and also that his father and mother have led a similar unstained life."
refashioned:
"Let us establish, then, that there must be temple attendants, priests, and priestesses. There must also be superintendents of roads and buildings, responsible both for preventing harm to people and for managing animals within the city and its outskirts. Three categories of officers are needed to serve the city's requirements.

Those overseeing the city shall be called city wardens. Those managing the marketplace shall be called market wardens. Those serving the temples shall be called priests. Any existing hereditary priesthoods shall be maintained undisturbed. But since a new city will likely have few or no hereditary priests, new priests and priestesses shall be appointed to serve those gods who currently lack attendants.

Some of these officers shall be elected and others chosen by lot, with citizens of every class mixing together amicably throughout the process, so that the state achieves the greatest possible unity of mind.

Temple officers specifically shall be chosen by lot, thereby entrusting their selection to God, who will direct the outcome as he sees fit. Anyone selected by lot must then pass a scrutiny: first, confirming that he is physically sound and of legitimate birth; and second, demonstrating that his family line is entirely pure, unstained by homicide or similar impiety in his own conduct, and that his parents have led similarly blameless lives."
elegant: Three categories of officers serve the city: city wardens for infrastructure, market wardens for the agora, and priests for temples. Hereditary priesthoods are preserved; new ones are created by lot, entrusting the choice to God. All lottery winners undergo scrutiny for physical soundness, legitimate birth, and family purity. The mix of election and lot across social classes promotes civic unity.
summary: Three categories of officials are established: city wardens, market wardens, and priests. Hereditary priesthoods are maintained, but new temple officers are chosen by lot to let God decide, then scrutinized for physical fitness, legitimacy, and family purity. The blend of election and lottery promotes social unity.
distilled: City wardens, market wardens, and priests are appointed through a combination of election and divine lot, with scrutiny ensuring physical soundness and family purity.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The three-fold administrative division (city, agora, temples) creates specialized governance for distinct civic spheres. The use of lot for priestly offices reflects genuine Greek practice and the Laws' theological commitments: divine selection through chance. The scrutiny requirements -- physical soundness, legitimate birth, family purity, no homicide -- parallel historical Athenian dokimasia procedures. The insistence on mixing classes 'in a friendly manner' to achieve unity of mind echoes Republic 462a-b on the unity of the ideal city. Cf. Republic 414d-415d on the 'noble lie' as a mechanism for social cohesion.

narrative:
"Now the laws about all divine things should be brought from Delphi, and interpreters appointed, under whose direction they should be used. The tenure of the priesthood should always be for a year and no longer; and he who will duly execute the sacred office, according to the laws of religion, must be not less than sixty years of age -- the laws shall be the same about priestesses.

As for the interpreters, they shall be appointed thus: Let the twelve tribes be distributed into groups of four, and let each group select four, one out of each tribe within the group, three times; and let the three who have the greatest number of votes out of the twelve appointed by each group, after undergoing a scrutiny, nine in all, be sent to Delphi, in order that the God may return one out of each triad; their age shall be the same as that of the priests, and the scrutiny of them shall be conducted in the same manner; let them be interpreters for life, and when any one dies let the four tribes select another from the tribe of the deceased.

Moreover, besides priests and interpreters, there must be treasurers, who will take charge of the property of the several temples, and of the sacred domains, and shall have authority over the produce and the letting of them; and three of them shall be chosen from the highest classes for the greater temples, and two for the lesser, and one for the least of all; the manner of their election and the scrutiny of them shall be the same as that of the generals. This shall be the order of the temples."
refashioned:
"All laws regarding divine matters should be obtained from Delphi, and interpreters should be appointed to direct their application. Priesthoods should be held for one year only, and no priest may be under sixty years of age. The same requirements apply to priestesses.

The interpreters shall be appointed as follows: the twelve tribes shall be arranged into three groups of four. Each group shall elect four interpreters, one from each tribe in the group, selecting three times. From the twelve thus chosen by each group, the three who receive the most votes, after passing scrutiny -- nine in total -- shall be sent to Delphi so that the God may select one from each set of three. These interpreters must meet the same age and scrutiny requirements as priests, and they shall serve for life. When one dies, the four tribes from his group shall select a replacement from the deceased's own tribe.

Beyond priests and interpreters, temple treasurers are also needed. They shall manage temple property and sacred lands, with authority over their produce and leasing arrangements. Three treasurers from the highest property classes shall serve the major temples, two for medium temples, and one for the smallest. Their election and scrutiny shall follow the same procedure as that of the generals. This shall be the complete order for temple administration."
elegant: Religious law comes from Delphi through appointed interpreters. Priests serve one-year terms and must be sixty or older. Nine lifetime interpreters are selected through tribal elections and Delphic oracle confirmation. Temple treasurers manage sacred property, with their number proportional to temple importance and their selection following the same rigorous process as military generals.
summary: Religious laws are sourced from Delphi through lifetime interpreters selected by tribal election and divine confirmation. Priests serve one-year terms from age sixty. Temple treasurers manage sacred property, elected with the same rigor as generals.
distilled: Religious authority flows from Delphi through lifetime interpreters, while priests serve annual terms and treasurers manage temple property, all subject to rigorous scrutiny.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. Delphi's role as the source of religious law reflects historical Greek practice and the Laws' deference to divine authority in sacred matters. The interpreters (exegetai) are a uniquely important office: selected through the most elaborate process in the Laws -- tribal election, scrutiny, then final selection by the Delphic oracle itself. Their lifetime tenure and divine confirmation make them the most authoritative religious officials. This contrasts sharply with the Republic, which has no formal religious apparatus. The age requirement of sixty for priests emphasizes reverence and experience. The proportional assignment of treasurers (3, 2, 1 by temple importance) is characteristic of the Laws' detailed institutional design.

narrative:
"Let everything have a guard as far as possible. Let the defence of the city be committed to the generals, and taxiarchs, and hipparchs, and phylarchs, and prytanes, and the wardens of the city, and of the agora, when the election of them has been completed.

The defence of the country shall be provided for as follows: The entire land has been already distributed into twelve as nearly as possible equal parts, and let the tribe allotted to a division provide annually for it five wardens of the country and commanders of the watch; and let each body of five have the power of selecting twelve others out of the youth of their own tribe -- these shall be not less than twenty-five years of age, and not more than thirty. And let there be allotted to them severally every month the various districts, in order that they may all acquire knowledge and experience of the whole country.

The term of service for commanders and for watchers shall continue during two years. After having had their stations allotted to them, they will go from place to place in regular order, making their round from left to right as their commanders direct them; (when I speak of going to the right, I mean that they are to go to the east). And at the commencement of the second year, in order that as many as possible of the guards may not only get a knowledge of the country at any one season of the year, but may also have experience of the manner in which different places are affected at different seasons of the year, their then commanders shall lead them again towards the left, from place to place in succession, until they have completed the second year."
refashioned:
"Let everything be guarded as thoroughly as possible. City defense shall be the responsibility of generals, taxiarchs, hipparchs, phylarchs, prytanes, and the city and market wardens, once all have been duly elected.

The defense of the countryside shall be organized as follows: since the land has already been divided into twelve roughly equal parts, each tribe assigned to a division shall annually provide five country wardens and watch commanders. Each group of five shall select twelve additional young men from their own tribe, between twenty-five and thirty years of age. These groups shall be rotated monthly through different districts so that every guard acquires thorough knowledge of the entire territory.

Service for both commanders and watchmen shall last two years. After receiving their station assignments, they shall patrol in regular rotation from west to east, as directed by their commanders. In the second year, the direction reverses -- the commanders lead them from east to west through successive stations -- ensuring that as many guards as possible experience not just different locations but how those locations change across different seasons of the year."
elegant: Every part of the state must have guardians. City defense falls to elected military and civic officials. The countryside is protected by tribal watch groups: five commanders select twelve young men (ages 25-30) per tribal district, rotating monthly through all territories over a two-year term. In the first year they patrol west to east; in the second, east to west -- ensuring complete knowledge of every region in every season.
summary: The Athenian establishes a comprehensive defense system. The city is guarded by elected officials, while the countryside is protected by rotating tribal watch groups of young men who patrol all twelve districts over two years, reversing direction each year to learn the terrain in all seasons.
distilled: Country defense is entrusted to rotating tribal watch groups of young men who patrol all districts over two years, gaining complete territorial knowledge across every season.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The country wardens (agroikoi phylakes) system is remarkably similar to the Athenian ephebeia -- the two-year military service required of young Athenian citizens. The rotational patrol system serves a dual purpose: military defense and civic education, as young men learn every part of their territory. The seasonal rotation (reversing direction in year two) is a sophisticated detail ensuring comprehensive knowledge. Cf. Republic 412d-414a on testing guardians through various experiences. This institution also recalls the 'secret police' (krypteia) of Sparta, mentioned later in 6.25, though the Laws' version is more educational than punitive.

narrative:
"In the third year other wardens of the country shall be chosen and commanders of the watch, five for each division, who are to be the superintendents of the bands of twelve. While on service at each station, their attention shall be directed to the following points:

In the first place, they shall see that the country is well protected against enemies; they shall trench and dig wherever this is required, and, as far as they can, they shall by fortifications keep off the evil-disposed, in order to prevent them from doing any harm to the country or the property; they shall use the beasts of burden and the labourers whom they find on the spot: these will be their instruments whom they will superintend, taking them, as far as possible, at the times when they are not engaged in their regular business.

They shall make every part of the country inaccessible to enemies, and as accessible as possible to friends; there shall be ways for man and beasts of burden and for cattle, and they shall take care to have them always as smooth as they can; and shall provide against the rains doing harm instead of good to the land, when they come down from the mountains into the hollow dells; and shall keep in the overflow by the help of works and ditches, in order that the valleys, receiving and drinking up the rain from heaven, and providing fountains and streams in the fields and regions which lie underneath, may furnish even to the dry places plenty of good water.

The fountains of water, whether of rivers or of springs, shall be ornamented with plantations and buildings for beauty; and let them bring together the streams in subterraneous channels, and make all things plenteous; and if there be a sacred grove or dedicated precinct in the neighbourhood, they shall conduct the water to the actual temples of the Gods, and so beautify them at all seasons of the year.

Everywhere in such places the youth shall make gymnasia for themselves, and warm baths for the aged, placing by them abundance of dry wood, for the benefit of those labouring under disease -- there the weary frame of the rustic, worn with toil, will receive a kindly welcome, far better than he would at the hands of a not over-wise doctor."
refashioned:
"In the third year, new country wardens and watch commanders shall be chosen -- five per division -- to supervise the bands of twelve. While serving at each station, they shall attend to the following duties:

First, they must ensure the country is well defended against enemies. They shall dig trenches and construct fortifications wherever needed to prevent the ill-intentioned from harming the land or property. They shall make use of the draft animals and laborers available locally, employing them as assistants and scheduling their service during times when they are free from their regular work.

They shall make the countryside inaccessible to enemies yet as accessible as possible to friends. Roads for people, pack animals, and livestock shall be maintained in the smoothest condition.
