They shall manage rainfall to prevent erosion, catching mountain runoff with embankments and channels so that valleys can absorb the rain, feeding fountains and streams to irrigate the fields below and bringing plentiful water even to the driest areas.

Water sources -- whether rivers or springs -- shall be beautified with plantings and structures. Streams shall be channeled underground where useful to maximize their benefit. If sacred groves or dedicated precincts stand nearby, water shall be directed to the temples to beautify them throughout the year.

In all such places, the young men shall build gymnasia for themselves and warm baths for the elderly, stocked with ample firewood. These shall especially serve those suffering from illness -- for a weary farmer, worn out by labor, will find better healing in such a place than from any mediocre physician."
elegant: Country wardens must fortify the land against enemies while keeping it accessible to friends. Roads must be maintained, rainfall managed through earthworks to prevent erosion and channel water to dry areas. Springs and rivers are beautified with plantings and buildings, with water directed to temples. The young men build gymnasia and warm baths for the elderly and sick -- rest and warm water being better medicine for a worn-out farmer than an unskilled doctor.
summary: Country wardens fortify the land, maintain roads, manage water resources through embankments and channels, beautify springs and temples with plantings, and build gymnasia and baths. These facilities serve both the young and the elderly sick, offering better healing than a mediocre physician.
distilled: Country wardens combine military fortification with infrastructure development -- managing roads, water, and public facilities that serve defense, beauty, and the health of both young and old.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage reveals the Laws' holistic vision of governance: military defense, agriculture, public works, aesthetics, and public health are integrated into a single administrative framework. The water management system -- catching mountain runoff, channeling it to dry areas, beautifying springs -- reflects practical Greek engineering wisdom. The preference for warm baths over physicians echoes Plato's skepticism about doctors in Republic 405c-408b and anticipates the Greek emphasis on preventive health through exercise and environment. The integration of sacred groves and temples into the water system unifies civic religion with practical infrastructure.

narrative:
"The building of these and the like works will be useful and ornamental; they will provide a pleasing amusement, but they will be a serious employment too; for the sixty wardens will have to guard their several divisions, not only with a view to enemies, but also with an eye to professing friends.

When a quarrel arises among neighbours or citizens, and any one, whether slave or freeman, wrongs another, let the five wardens decide small matters on their own authority; but where the charge against another relates to greater matters, the seventeen composed of the fives and twelves, shall determine any charges which one man brings against another, not involving more than three minae.

Every judge and magistrate shall be liable to give an account of his conduct in office, except those who, like kings, have the final decision.

Moreover, as regards the aforesaid wardens of the country, if they do any wrong to those of whom they have the care, whether by imposing upon them unequal tasks, or by taking the produce of the soil or implements of husbandry without their consent; also if they receive anything in the way of a bribe, or decide suits unjustly, or if they yield to the influences of flattery, let them be publicly dishonoured; and in regard to any other wrong which they do to the inhabitants of the country, if the question be of a mina, let them submit to the decision of the villagers in the neighbourhood; but in suits of greater amount, or in case of lesser, if they refuse to submit, trusting that their monthly removal into another part of the country will enable them to escape -- in such cases the injured party may bring his suit in the common court, and if he obtain a verdict he may exact from the defendant, who refused to submit, a double penalty."
refashioned:
"Building these works will be both useful and beautiful -- a pleasant occupation but also a serious duty. The sixty wardens must guard their divisions not only against enemies but also against those who claim to be friends.

When disputes arise between neighbors or citizens, and someone -- whether slave or free -- wrongs another, the five wardens shall decide minor cases on their own authority. For more serious matters involving claims up to three minae, the combined body of seventeen -- the five wardens plus their twelve assistants -- shall adjudicate.

Every judge and magistrate must be held accountable for their conduct in office, except those who hold supreme judicial authority.

As for the country wardens themselves, if they wrong those under their care -- by assigning unequal burdens, seizing farm produce or tools without consent, accepting bribes, judging cases unjustly, or yielding to flattery -- they shall be publicly disgraced. For other offenses against country inhabitants: cases involving up to a mina shall be judged by the local villagers. For larger amounts, or for smaller cases where the warden refuses to submit to local judgment, hoping that his monthly transfer to another district will let him escape accountability -- in such cases, the injured party may bring suit in the common court, and upon winning may exact double the penalty from the warden who refused to face judgment locally."
elegant: The sixty wardens serve as both builders and judges. Minor disputes are decided by the five wardens; larger cases by the combined seventeen. All magistrates must account for their conduct. Wardens who abuse their power through unequal burdens, theft, bribery, unjust decisions, or susceptibility to flattery face public disgrace. Those who evade local judgment by exploiting their monthly rotation face double penalties in the common court. Accountability is inescapable.
summary: Country wardens both develop infrastructure and adjudicate disputes, with minor cases decided by five and larger ones by seventeen. All officials must account for their conduct. Wardens who abuse their authority face public disgrace, and those who try to evade justice through their monthly rotation face double penalties.
distilled: Wardens serve as both developers and judges with strict accountability -- abuses bring public disgrace, and attempts to evade judgment through rotation result in double penalties.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The accountability provisions are remarkably detailed: the Laws anticipates specific forms of corruption (unequal tasks, seizure of property, bribes, unjust verdicts, flattery) and designs countermeasures. The double penalty for evading local jurisdiction by exploiting rotation is a clever anti-corruption mechanism. The principle that all magistrates must give account (euthyna) reflects actual Athenian practice. The exception for those with 'final decision' parallels the Republic's philosopher-kings who are accountable only to reason itself. Cf. Republic 412d-e on testing guardians against corruption.

narrative:
"The wardens and the overseers of the country, while on their two years' service, shall have common meals at their several stations, and shall all live together; and he who is absent from the common meal, or sleeps out, if only for one day or night, unless by order of his commanders, or by reason of absolute necessity, if the five denounce him and inscribe his name in the agora as not having kept his guard, let him be deemed to have betrayed the city, as far as lay in his power, and let him be disgraced and beaten with impunity by any one who meets him and is willing to punish him.

If any of the commanders is guilty of such an irregularity, the whole company of sixty shall see to it, and he who is cognizant of the offence, and does not bring the offender to trial, shall be amenable to the same laws as the younger offender himself, and shall pay a heavier fine, and be incapable of ever commanding the young.

The guardians of the law are to be careful inspectors of these matters, and shall either prevent or punish offenders. Every man should remember the universal rule, that he who is not a good servant will not be a good master; a man should pride himself more upon serving well than upon commanding well: first upon serving the laws, which is also the service of the Gods; in the second place, upon having served ancient and honourable men in the days of his youth."
refashioned:
"During their two years of service, the wardens and overseers of the country shall eat communal meals at their stations and live together. Anyone absent from the common meal or sleeping elsewhere, even for a single day or night, without the commander's order or absolute necessity, shall -- if the five denounce him and post his name in the agora as having deserted his guard -- be considered to have betrayed the city to the extent of his power. He shall be disgraced and may be beaten without consequence by anyone who encounters him and chooses to administer the punishment.

If a commander commits such an offense, the entire company of sixty is responsible for addressing it. Any member who knows of the offense and fails to bring the offender to trial shall be subject to the same penalties as the younger offender, shall pay a heavier fine, and shall be permanently barred from ever commanding young men again.

The guardians of the law shall carefully inspect these matters, preventing or punishing violations. Everyone should remember the universal principle: he who has not learned to serve well will never command well. A person should take greater pride in serving excellently than in commanding -- first in serving the laws, which is also service to the Gods, and second in having served honorable elders during one's youth."
elegant: Country wardens must live and eat communally during their two-year service. Unauthorized absence, even for one day, constitutes betrayal of the city, punishable by public disgrace and physical correction. Commanders who violate this rule face harsher penalties, and those who cover for them share their guilt and are permanently barred from command. The deeper principle: good leadership requires first learning to serve -- serving the laws (which is serving the Gods) and serving one's elders.
summary: Country wardens must maintain strict communal discipline during service, with unauthorized absence treated as betrayal. Commanders face even harsher penalties, and cover-ups are punished equally. The underlying principle is that good command requires first learning good service -- to the laws, to the gods, and to one's elders.
distilled: Good leadership requires first learning to serve -- the laws, the gods, and one's elders -- and communal military discipline enforces this principle through strict accountability.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The maxim 'he who is not a good servant will not be a good master' is one of the Laws' most quoted principles. It directly inverts the typical Greek aristocratic attitude and anticipates the servant-leadership concept. The communal meals (syssitia) echo Spartan practice, which Plato admired. The severe punishment for absence -- public disgrace and beating -- reflects military discipline. The chain of accountability (wardens -> commanders -> company of sixty -> guardians of the law) creates a nested enforcement system. Cf. Republic 412e-413d on testing guardians through hardship, and the Spartan krypteia for comparison with this rural patrol system.

narrative:
"Furthermore, during the two years in which any one is a warden of the country, his daily food ought to be of a simple and humble kind. When the twelve have been chosen, let them and the five meet together, and determine that they will be their own servants, and, like servants, will not have other slaves and servants for their own use, neither will they use those of the villagers and husbandmen for their private advantage, but for the public service only; and in general they should make up their minds to live independently by themselves, servants of each other and of themselves.

Further, at all seasons of the year, summer and winter alike, let them be under arms and survey minutely the whole country; thus they will at once keep guard, and at the same time acquire a perfect knowledge of every locality. There can be no more important kind of information than the exact knowledge of a man's own country; and for this as well as for more general reasons of pleasure and advantage, hunting with dogs and other kinds of sports should be pursued by the young.

The service to whom this is committed may be called the secret police, or wardens of the country; the name does not much signify, but every one who has the safety of the state at heart will use his utmost diligence in this service."
refashioned:
"Furthermore, during their two years as country wardens, their daily food should be simple and modest. When the twelve assistants are chosen, they and the five commanders should agree to serve themselves: they will not use slaves or servants for their personal needs, nor will they commandeer the villagers' or farmers' workers for private purposes -- only for public service. In general, they should resolve to live independently, serving one another and themselves.

In every season -- summer and winter alike -- they shall remain under arms and survey the entire countryside in detail. This serves a dual purpose: maintaining guard while acquiring thorough knowledge of every locality. There is no more valuable information than intimate familiarity with one's own territory. For this reason, as well as for the general benefits of pleasure and fitness, the young men should pursue hunting with dogs and other outdoor activities.

This service might be called the secret police, or simply the country wardens -- the name matters little. What matters is that everyone who cares about the state's safety devotes their fullest energy to this duty."
elegant: Country wardens live simply, serve themselves without slaves, and use local labor only for public purposes. Year-round and under arms, they survey every part of the territory -- for no knowledge is more valuable than intimate familiarity with one's own land. Hunting and outdoor sport complement this military-geographic education. Call them secret police or country wardens; the name matters less than the devotion every patriot owes this service.
summary: Country wardens live simply, serve themselves, and patrol the territory year-round under arms, building intimate knowledge of the land through both military survey and hunting. The Athenian calls this service the most important form of civic knowledge, regardless of its formal name.
distilled: Country wardens live austerely, serve themselves, and patrol year-round to acquire the most valuable civic knowledge: intimate familiarity with every part of their own territory.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The self-service requirement -- no slaves for personal use -- is a striking egalitarian measure within an institution that otherwise maintains slavery. The emphasis on geographic knowledge recalls the Republic's requirement that guardians know their city thoroughly (Republic 415d-416a). The hunting recommendation connects to Laws Book 7's extensive treatment of proper and improper hunting. The term 'secret police' (krypteia) directly references the Spartan institution where young men were sent into the countryside to surveil and sometimes kill helots. The Laws transforms this brutal Spartan practice into something more educational and constructive, characteristic of its approach to Spartan institutions throughout.

narrative:
"After the wardens of the country, we have to speak of the election of wardens of the agora and of the city. The wardens of the country were sixty in number, and the wardens of the city will be three, and will divide the twelve parts of the city into three; like the former, they shall have care of the ways, and of the different high roads which lead out of the country into the city, and of the buildings, that they may be all made according to law; also of the waters, which the guardians of the supply preserve and convey to them, care being taken that they may reach the fountains pure and abundant, and be both an ornament and a benefit to the city.

These also should be men of influence, and at leisure to take care of the public interest. Let every man propose as warden of the city any one whom he likes out of the highest class, and when the vote has been given on them, and the number is reduced to the six who have the greatest number of votes, let the electing officers choose by lot three out of the six, and when they have undergone a scrutiny let them hold office according to the laws laid down for them.

Next, let the wardens of the agora be elected in like manner, out of the first and second class, five in number: ten are to be first elected, and out of the ten five are to be chosen by lot, as in the election of the wardens of the city -- these when they have undergone a scrutiny are to be declared magistrates.

Every one shall vote for every one, and he who will not vote, if he be informed against before the magistrates, shall be fined fifty drachmae, and shall also be deemed a bad citizen. Let any one who likes go to the assembly and to the general council; it shall be compulsory to go on citizens of the first and second class, and they shall pay a fine of ten drachmae if they be found not answering to their names at the assembly. The third and fourth class shall be under no compulsion, and shall be let off without a fine, unless the magistrates have commanded all to be present, in consequence of some urgent necessity."
refashioned:
"After the country wardens, we must discuss electing wardens for the agora and the city. There were sixty country wardens; the city wardens shall be three, dividing the twelve city districts into three zones. Like their rural counterparts, they shall oversee roads, highways connecting city and countryside, and buildings to ensure legal compliance. They shall also manage the water supply, ensuring that what the supply guardians deliver reaches the fountains pure and plentiful, serving as both a civic ornament and public benefit.

These should be influential men with sufficient leisure for public service. Any citizen may nominate a candidate from the highest class. After voting narrows the field to six, the electing officers choose three by lot, who then undergo scrutiny and hold office according to the prescribed laws.

Five market wardens shall be elected similarly from the first and second classes: ten are initially elected, then five chosen by lot, as with the city wardens. After scrutiny, they are declared magistrates.

Every citizen must vote for every office. Anyone who refuses and is reported to the magistrates shall be fined fifty drachmae and labeled a bad citizen. Attendance at the assembly and general council is voluntary for lower classes but compulsory for the first and second classes, who face a ten-drachmae fine for absence. The third and fourth classes attend without compulsion and face no penalty, unless the magistrates declare a special session due to urgent necessity."
elegant: Three city wardens oversee roads, buildings, and water supply across the twelve districts. Five market wardens manage the agora. Both are selected through nomination, voting, lottery, and scrutiny -- combining democratic participation with meritocratic filtering. Universal voting is compulsory under penalty. Assembly attendance is mandatory for the wealthiest two classes but voluntary for the lower two, unless an emergency is declared. Wealth brings not just privilege but heightened civic obligation.
summary: Three city wardens and five market wardens are elected through nomination, voting, lottery, and scrutiny. Universal voting is compulsory with fines for abstention. Assembly attendance is mandatory for wealthy classes but optional for lower classes, reflecting the principle that greater wealth demands greater civic participation.
distilled: City and market wardens are elected through a combined process of vote and lot, with compulsory voting for all citizens and compulsory assembly attendance increasing with wealth class.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The graduated civic obligations -- compulsory assembly for wealthy classes, voluntary for poorer ones -- inverts modern assumptions about democratic participation. The Laws treats civic engagement as a duty that increases with means, reflecting the proportional equality principle from 6.15-16. The four-stage selection process (nomination, voting, lottery, scrutiny) is the Laws' signature mechanism, blending democratic, oligarchic, and meritocratic elements. The infrastructure focus -- roads, buildings, water supply -- shows the Laws' attention to material governance. Cf. Republic 420b-421c on whether guardians should be 'happy' or serve the whole.

narrative: "The wardens of the agora shall observe the order appointed by law for the agora, and shall have the charge of the temples and fountains which are in the agora; and they shall see that no one injures anything, and punish him who does, with stripes and bonds, if he be a slave or stranger; but if he be a citizen who misbehaves in this way, they shall have the power themselves of inflicting a fine upon him to the amount of a hundred drachmae, or with the consent of the wardens of the city up to double that amount. And let the wardens of the city have a similar power of imposing punishments and fines in their own department; and let them impose fines by their own authority, up to a mina, or up to two minae with the consent of the wardens of the agora."
refashioned:
"The market wardens shall enforce the legal order of the agora and take charge of the temples and fountains within it. They must ensure that nothing is damaged, punishing offenders appropriately: slaves and foreigners with physical punishment and detention, but citizens with fines. Market wardens may fine a misbehaving citizen up to one hundred drachmae on their own authority, or up to double that amount with the consent of the city wardens.

City wardens shall have corresponding powers in their own jurisdiction: they may impose fines up to one mina on their own authority, or up to two minae with the consent of the market wardens."
elegant: Market wardens enforce order in the agora and protect its temples and fountains. Punishment varies by status: slaves and foreigners face physical correction; citizens face fines. Both market and city wardens may impose fines within limits, with mutual consent required for larger penalties -- creating cooperative oversight between jurisdictions.
summary: Market wardens enforce agora order and protect its sacred and public spaces. Punishments differ by status: physical for slaves and foreigners, fines for citizens. Both market and city wardens can impose fines within set limits, with mutual consent needed for larger amounts.
distilled: Market and city wardens enforce order through graduated punishments -- physical for non-citizens, monetary for citizens -- with cooperative authority over larger fines.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The differential punishment by status (stripes for slaves/foreigners, fines for citizens) reflects standard Greek legal practice. The mutual consent requirement for larger fines -- market wardens need city wardens' agreement and vice versa -- creates a system of checks and balances at the local level, preventing any single magistrate from wielding excessive punitive power. This cooperative model is characteristic of the Laws' approach to institutional design: overlapping jurisdictions with built-in checks. Cf. Republic 433a-b on justice as each doing their proper function.

narrative:
"In the next place, it will be proper to appoint directors of music and gymnastic, two kinds of each -- of the one kind the business will be education, of the other, the superintendence of contests. In speaking of education, the law means to speak of those who have the care of order and instruction in gymnasia and schools, and of the going to school, and of school buildings for boys and girls; and in speaking of contests, the law refers to the judges of gymnastics and of music; these again are divided into two classes, the one having to do with music, the other with gymnastics; and the same who judge of the gymnastic contests of men, shall judge of horses; but in music there shall be one set of judges of solo singing, and of imitation -- I mean of rhapsodists, players on the harp, the flute and the like, and another who shall judge of choral song.

First of all, we must choose directors for the choruses of boys, and men, and maidens, whom they shall follow in the amusement of the dance, and for our other musical arrangements; one director will be enough for the choruses, and he should be not less than forty years of age. One director will also be enough to introduce the solo singers, and to give judgment on the competitors, and he ought not to be less than thirty years of age."
refashioned:
"Next, we should appoint directors of music and gymnastics, two types of each. One type handles education, the other oversees competitive contests.

By education, the law means those responsible for order and instruction in gymnasia and schools -- overseeing attendance, school facilities, and the education of both boys and girls. By contests, the law means judges who evaluate athletic and musical competitions. The contest judges are further divided into two groups: one for gymnastics and one for music. Those who judge men's athletic competitions shall also judge equestrian events. For music, there shall be separate judges for solo performance -- rhapsodists, harp players, flute players, and similar artists -- and for choral singing.

First, we must select directors for the choruses of boys, men, and young women, who shall lead them in dance and other musical activities. One director suffices for all choruses, and he must be at least forty years old. One director also suffices for solo performances, responsible for presenting competitors and rendering judgment; he must be at least thirty years old."
elegant: Directors of music and gymnastics are needed for two purposes: education and competitive contests. Educational directors oversee schools and gymnasia for boys and girls. Contest judges are divided between athletics (including equestrian events) and music (solo performance versus choral song). One chorus director aged forty or older leads all choral activities; one solo performance director aged thirty or older manages competitions.
summary: The Athenian establishes directors for music and gymnastics, divided between education and contests. Contest judges handle athletics (including horses) or music (solo versus choral). A chorus director must be at least forty; a solo performance director at least thirty.
distilled: Directors of music and gymnastics serve dual functions -- education and contest supervision -- with age requirements ensuring maturity in leadership of the arts.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The equal treatment of boys and girls in education is notable for ancient Greece, though the Laws is less radical than the Republic's full gender equality for guardians (Republic 451c-457b). The distinction between educational and competitive roles for musical/athletic directors reflects the Laws' understanding that these arts serve two purposes: character formation and public performance. The age requirements (40 for choral, 30 for solo) suggest that choral leadership requires greater maturity than solo performance direction. Cf. Republic 376e-403c on the role of music and gymnastics in guardian education.

narrative:
"The director and manager of the choruses shall be elected after the following manner: Let any persons who commonly take an interest in such matters go to the meeting, and be fined if they do not go (the guardians of the law shall judge of their fault), but those who have no interest shall not be compelled. The elector shall propose as director some one who understands music, and he in the scrutiny may be challenged on the one part by those who say he has no skill, and defended on the other hand by those who say that he has.

Ten are to be elected by vote, and he of the ten who is chosen by lot shall undergo a scrutiny, and lead the choruses for a year according to law. And in like manner the competitor who wins the lot shall be leader of the solo and concert music for that year; and he who is thus elected shall deliver the award to the judges.

In the next place, we have to choose judges in the contests of horses and of men; these shall be selected from the third and also from the second class of citizens, and three first classes shall be compelled to go to the election, but the lowest may stay away with impunity; and let there be three elected by lot out of the twenty who have been chosen previously, and they must also have the vote and approval of the examiners. But if any one is rejected in the scrutiny at any ballot or decision, others shall be chosen in the same manner, and undergo a similar scrutiny."
refashioned:
"The chorus director shall be elected as follows: anyone who regularly takes an interest in such matters must attend the meeting, facing a fine if absent -- the guardians of the law shall judge any delinquency. Those without interest in music are not compelled to attend. Electors shall nominate candidates who understand music; during scrutiny, opponents may challenge the nominee's competence while supporters defend it.

Ten candidates are elected by vote, and one of the ten is chosen by lot, undergoes scrutiny, and leads the choruses for one year according to law. The solo and concert music director is selected the same way: the lot-winner leads for one year and delivers awards to the judges.

Next, judges for athletic and equestrian contests shall be selected from the second and third property classes. The top three classes must attend the election; the lowest class may abstain without penalty. Twenty candidates are first elected, then three are chosen by lot from those twenty, and these must also receive the approval of the examiners. If anyone fails scrutiny at any stage, replacements are chosen and examined by the same method."
elegant: Musical directors are elected by those with genuine interest in the arts -- compelled to attend, while the indifferent are excused. Ten nominees are voted on, one chosen by lot and scrutinized for the annual post. Athletic judges come from the middle property classes, chosen by vote then lot then scrutiny. Replacements follow the same rigorous process. The system ensures that only the genuinely competent and approved lead cultural and athletic life.
summary: Musical directors are elected by interested citizens through a process of nomination, voting, lottery, and scrutiny, serving for one year. Athletic judges are selected from middle classes through the same multi-stage process. Any candidate failing scrutiny is replaced through identical procedures.
distilled: Directors of music and athletic judges are selected through vote, lot, and scrutiny, with compulsory participation by those with relevant interest and expertise.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The requirement that those 'who commonly take an interest' must attend, while the uninterested may abstain, is a pragmatic compromise: it ensures informed voters without forcing the ignorant to make blind choices. This principle -- expertise-weighted participation -- distinguishes the Laws' approach from pure democracy. The multi-stage process (vote -> lot -> scrutiny) remains consistent across all offices. The middle-class selection base for athletic judges prevents both elite monopoly and unqualified selection. Cf. Republic 401b-402a on the importance of proper artistic judgment for the state.

narrative:
"There remains the minister of the education of youth, male and female; he too will rule according to law; one such minister will be sufficient, and he must be fifty years old, and have children lawfully begotten, both boys and girls by preference, at any rate, one or the other.

He who is elected, and he who is the elector, should consider that of all the great offices of state, this is the greatest; for the first shoot of any plant, if it makes a good start towards the attainment of its natural excellence, has the greatest effect on its maturity; and this is not only true of plants, but of animals wild and tame, and also of men. Man, as we say, is a tame or civilized animal; nevertheless, he requires proper instruction and a fortunate nature, and then of all animals he becomes the most divine and most civilized; but if he be insufficiently or ill educated he is the most savage of earthly creatures.

Wherefore the legislator ought not to allow the education of children to become a secondary or accidental matter. In the first place, he who would be rightly provident about them, should begin by taking care that he is elected, who of all the citizens is in every way best; him the legislator shall do his utmost to appoint guardian and superintendent.

To this end all the magistrates, with the exception of the council and prytanes, shall go to the temple of Apollo, and elect by ballot him of the guardians of the law whom they severally think will be the best superintendent of education. And he who has the greatest number of votes, after he has undergone a scrutiny at the hands of all the magistrates who have been his electors, with the exception of the guardians of the law -- shall hold office for five years; and in the sixth year let another be chosen in like manner to fill his office."
refashioned:
"There remains the minister of education for young people, both male and female. One minister is sufficient, ruling according to law. He must be at least fifty years old and must have legitimate children -- preferably both sons and daughters, though at minimum one or the other.

Both the electors and the elected should understand that of all the great offices of state, this is the greatest. The first growth of any plant, if it starts well toward its natural potential, has the greatest impact on its eventual maturity. This applies not only to plants but to animals both wild and domesticated, and to human beings as well. Humans are, as we say, the civilized animal. Yet with proper instruction and a fortunate nature, humans become the most divine and civilized of all creatures; insufficiently or poorly educated, they become the most savage things on earth.

Therefore, the legislator must never treat children's education as secondary or incidental. The first step toward proper provision for children is ensuring that the very best citizen is chosen as their guardian and superintendent. The legislator must do everything in his power to make this appointment correctly.

To this end, all magistrates except the council and prytanes shall go to the temple of Apollo and vote by ballot for whichever guardian of the law they believe would be the best superintendent of education. The candidate with the most votes, after undergoing scrutiny by all the magistrates who elected him except the guardians of the law, shall serve for five years. In the sixth year, a successor shall be chosen by the same process."
elegant: The minister of education is the greatest office in the state. Just as the first growth of a plant determines its maturity, so the early formation of human character determines everything that follows. Humans are uniquely capable of becoming either the most divine of creatures through proper education or the most savage through its absence. Therefore the very best citizen must be chosen as superintendent of education -- elected by all magistrates at the temple of Apollo, scrutinized, and appointed for five years.
summary: The minister of education is declared the most important office in the state, since early formation determines character just as a plant's first growth determines its maturity. Properly educated humans become the most divine of creatures; poorly educated, the most savage. The best citizen must be chosen, elected by magistrates and serving five years.
distilled: Education is the greatest office of state because a human being's first formation, like a plant's first growth, determines whether they become the most divine or most savage of creatures.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage contains one of Plato's most powerful statements on education. The declaration that the superintendent of education holds the greatest office surpasses even the guardians of the law in importance, reflecting the Laws' conviction that character formation is the foundation of political life. The plant metaphor for early education appears also at Republic 377a-b. The paradox of human nature -- potentially the most divine or most savage -- echoes Republic 589a-b and Protagoras 321c-322d. The requirement for the minister to have children reflects practical wisdom: personal experience of parenthood informs educational judgment. The five-year term (longest in the Laws) reflects the office's gravity. Cf. Republic 376c-412a on the education of guardians as the Republic's central concern.

narrative: "If any one dies while he is holding a public office, and more than thirty days before his term of office expires, let those whose business it is elect another to the office in the same manner as before. And if any one who is entrusted with orphans dies, let the relations both on the father's and mother's side, who are residing at home, including cousins, appoint another guardian within ten days, or be fined a drachma a day for neglect to do so."
refashioned: "If any officeholder dies more than thirty days before his term expires, those responsible for elections shall fill the vacancy using the same procedures as the original appointment. If a guardian of orphans dies, relatives on both the father's and mother's side -- including cousins -- who reside locally must appoint a replacement guardian within ten days or face a daily fine of one drachma for each day of neglect."
elegant: When an officeholder dies more than thirty days before his term ends, a replacement is elected by the same process. When a guardian of orphans dies, relatives from both sides must appoint a successor within ten days or pay a daily fine. Continuity of care and governance must never lapse.
summary: Vacancies in public office are filled by the standard election process if more than thirty days remain in the term. Orphans' guardians who die must be replaced by relatives within ten days, with daily fines for delay.
distilled: Public office vacancies are filled by standard procedures, and guardians of orphans must be replaced by relatives within ten days or face daily fines.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This brief but important provision addresses institutional continuity. The thirty-day threshold for replacement is practical -- shorter vacancies are not worth the disruption of a full election. The orphan guardianship provision reflects the Laws' deep concern for children's welfare, consistent with the emphasis on education as the greatest office (6.30). The daily fine creates an escalating incentive for prompt action. Orphan protection was a serious matter in Greek law -- cf. Athenian practices where the archon had special responsibility for orphans and widows. The requirement that relatives from both paternal and maternal sides participate broadens the support network.

narrative:
"A city which has no regular courts of law ceases to be a city; and again, if a judge is silent and says no more in preliminary proceedings than the litigants, as is the case in arbitrations, he will never be able to decide justly; wherefore a multitude of judges will not easily judge well, nor a few if they are bad. The point in dispute between the parties should be made clear; and time, and deliberation, and repeated examination, greatly tend to clear up doubts.

For this reason, he who goes to law with another should go first of all to his neighbours and friends who know best the questions at issue. And if he be unable to obtain from them a satisfactory decision, let him have recourse to another court; and if the two courts cannot settle the matter, let a third put an end to the suit."
refashioned:
"A city without regular courts of law is no city at all. At the same time, if a judge stays silent during preliminary proceedings and contributes no more than the litigants themselves -- as happens in arbitrations -- he will never reach just decisions. Therefore neither a large number of judges nor a small number of poor ones will produce good justice. The essential thing is to make the disputed point clear between the parties, since time, careful deliberation, and repeated examination are the greatest aids to resolving doubts.

For this reason, anyone bringing a legal dispute should first go to neighbors and friends who best understand the matter at issue. If they cannot provide a satisfactory resolution, let him proceed to a second court. And if that court also cannot settle the dispute, let a third and final court bring the matter to a close."
elegant: Without courts, a city is no city. But courts must be properly structured: passive judges who merely listen, as in arbitration, cannot decide justly. Clarity, time, deliberation, and repeated examination are essential for just outcomes. Disputes should first go to neighbors who know the situation best, then escalate through two higher courts if needed -- three levels ensuring that every dispute finds resolution.
summary: Courts are essential to any city, but justice requires active judges, clear presentation of issues, and sufficient time for deliberation. Disputes should be resolved first by knowledgeable neighbors, then escalate through two additional courts if needed.
distilled: Justice requires active judges with time for deliberation, and disputes should escalate through three court levels -- neighbors first, then progressively higher tribunals.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The three-tiered court system (neighbors -> second court -> final court) creates a practical dispute-resolution ladder that begins with informal, local knowledge and escalates to formal authority. The critique of passive judges and arbitration is significant: the Laws wants judges who actively investigate and question, not mere arbiters. This reflects Plato's broader epistemological commitment -- truth emerges through active dialectical inquiry, not passive listening. Cf. Republic 405a-b on the proliferation of lawsuits as a sign of civic illness, and Statesman 305b-c on the relationship between judgment and political authority.

narrative:
"Now the establishment of courts of justice may be regarded as a choice of magistrates, for every magistrate must also be a judge of some things; and the judge, though he be not a magistrate, yet in certain respects is a very important magistrate on the day on which he is determining a suit. Regarding then the judges also as magistrates, let us say who are fit to be judges, and of what they are to be judges, and how many of them are to judge in each suit.

Let that be the supreme tribunal which the litigants appoint in common for themselves, choosing certain persons by agreement. And let there be two other tribunals: one for private causes, when a citizen accuses another of wronging him and wishes to get a decision; the other for public causes, in which some citizen is of opinion that the public has been wronged by an individual, and is willing to vindicate the common interests. And we must not forget to mention how the judges are to be qualified, and who they are to be."
refashioned:
"Establishing courts of justice is essentially a form of choosing magistrates, since every magistrate must act as a judge in some matters. And while a judge may not formally be a magistrate, on the day he determines a case he functions as a very important one. So let us treat judges as magistrates and consider who is qualified to judge, what cases they should hear, and how many judges should sit on each case.

The supreme tribunal shall be one that both litigants agree to form, choosing judges by mutual consent. Beyond this, there shall be two additional courts: one for private disputes, where a citizen accuses another of personal wrong and seeks a ruling; and one for public offenses, where a citizen believes an individual has wronged the community and is willing to champion the public interest. We must also specify the qualifications judges must meet and who they shall be."
elegant: Courts are a form of magistracy, since judges exercise magistrate-like authority when deciding cases. Three tribunals are needed: a supreme tribunal formed by mutual agreement of the parties; a court for private disputes between citizens; and a court for public offenses where individuals have wronged the community. The qualifications and number of judges for each must be carefully specified.
summary: The Athenian treats courts as a species of magistracy, since judges exercise real authority. He establishes three tribunals: a supreme court chosen by the parties, a private court for personal disputes, and a public court for offenses against the community.
distilled: Three tribunals are needed: a mutually agreed supreme court, a private court for personal disputes, and a public court for offenses against the community.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The insight that judges are effectively magistrates on the day they decide cases is characteristic of Plato's functional analysis of political roles. The three-court system separates consensual dispute resolution (supreme tribunal), private litigation, and public prosecution -- a sophisticated jurisdictional division. The public cause provision, where a citizen 'vindicates the common interests,' anticipates the modern concept of standing in public interest litigation. Cf. Republic 433e on justice as each person doing their proper function, applied here to judicial specialization.

narrative:
"In the first place, let there be a tribunal open to all private persons who are trying causes one against another for the third time, and let this be composed as follows: All the officers of state, as well annual as those holding office for a longer period, when the new year is about to commence, in the month following after the summer solstice, on the last day but one of the year, shall meet in some temple, and calling God to witness, shall dedicate one judge from every magistracy to be their first-fruits, choosing in each office him who seems to them to be the best, and whom they deem likely to decide the causes of his fellow-citizens during the ensuing year in the best and holiest manner.

And when the election is completed, a scrutiny shall be held in the presence of the electors themselves, and if any one be rejected another shall be chosen in the same manner. Those who have undergone the scrutiny shall judge the causes of those who have declined the inferior courts, and shall give their vote openly.

The councillors and other magistrates who have elected them shall be required to be hearers and spectators of the causes; and any one else may be present who pleases. If one man charges another with having intentionally decided wrong, let him go to the guardians of the law and lay his accusation before them, and he who is found guilty in such a case shall pay damages to the injured party equal to half the injury; but if he shall appear to deserve a greater penalty, the judges shall determine what additional punishment he shall suffer, and how much more he ought to pay to the public treasury, and to the party who brought the suit."
refashioned:
"First, there shall be a tribunal for all private persons whose cases have reached a third hearing. It shall be constituted as follows: all officeholders -- whether serving annual or longer terms -- shall gather in a temple on the second-to-last day of the year, in the month following the summer solstice. There, calling God as witness, they shall dedicate one judge from each magistracy as their 'first-fruits,' selecting from each office the person they consider best qualified and most likely to judge their fellow citizens' cases in the holiest and most just manner during the coming year.

After election, scrutiny shall be conducted in the electors' presence. Any rejected candidate shall be replaced through the same process. The approved judges shall hear cases that lower courts could not resolve, and they shall cast their votes openly.

The councillors and magistrates who elected them must attend as observers, and any other citizen may attend as well. If anyone accuses a judge of having intentionally rendered a wrong decision, he shall bring the charge before the guardians of the law. A judge found guilty shall pay the injured party damages equal to half the harm. If the offense warrants greater punishment, the judges shall determine the additional penalty, specifying payments both to the public treasury and to the accuser."
elegant: The highest private tribunal is formed annually through a sacred ceremony: on the year's penultimate day, all magistrates gather in a temple to select the best judge from each office as a 'first-fruit' offering to justice. These judges hear cases that lower courts failed to resolve, voting openly while magistrates observe. Judges found guilty of intentional wrongdoing pay half the damages to the injured party, with additional penalties at the court's discretion.
summary: The supreme private tribunal is constituted annually through a sacred ceremony where magistrates select their best members as judges. These judges hear third-instance cases with open voting and mandatory public observation. Corrupt judges face damages and additional penalties.
distilled: The highest court is formed annually when magistrates dedicate their best members as judges in a sacred ceremony, with open voting and penalties for judicial corruption.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The 'first-fruits' (aparchai) language is remarkable -- dedicating judges as sacred offerings transforms judicial selection into a religious act, reflecting the Laws' pervasive integration of politics and theology. Open voting ensures transparency, while mandatory observation by electing magistrates creates ongoing accountability. The penalties for intentional judicial wrongdoing (half-damages plus discretionary additional punishment) address a perennial problem in legal systems. The summer solstice timing anchors the civic calendar to the cosmic order. Cf. Republic 360b-362a (Ring of Gyges) on whether judges and rulers would be just without oversight -- the Laws' answer is institutional accountability rather than philosophical virtue alone.

narrative:
"In the judgment of offences against the state, the people ought to participate, for when any one wrongs the state all are wronged, and may reasonably complain if they are not allowed to share in the decision. Such causes ought to originate with the people, and they ought also to have the final decision of them, but the trial of them shall take place before three of the highest magistrates, upon whom the plaintiff and the defendant shall agree; and if they are not able to come to an agreement themselves, the council shall choose one of the two proposed.

And in private suits, too, as far as is possible, all should have a share; for he who has no share in the administration of justice, is apt to imagine that he has no share in the state at all. And for this reason there shall be a court of law in every tribe, and the judges shall be chosen by lot; they shall give their decisions at once, and shall be inaccessible to entreaties.

The final judgment shall rest with that court which, as we maintain, has been established in the most incorruptible form of which human things admit: this shall be the court established for those who are unable to get rid of their suits either in the courts of neighbours or of the tribes."
refashioned:
"When it comes to offenses against the state, the people must participate in judgment. When someone wrongs the state, every citizen is wronged, and they have a legitimate grievance if excluded from the decision. Such cases should originate with the people and receive their final verdict. However, the actual trial shall be conducted before three of the highest magistrates agreed upon by both plaintiff and defendant. If the parties cannot agree, the council shall select from between the two proposed candidates.

In private suits as well, everyone should participate to the greatest extent possible. A person who has no role in administering justice will naturally feel that he has no stake in the state at all. For this reason, every tribe shall have its own court of law, with judges chosen by lot. These judges shall render immediate decisions and remain immune to outside pressure.

The final court of appeal shall be the one we have established in the most incorruptible form humanly achievable. This court shall serve those whose disputes could not be resolved in either the neighborhood courts or the tribal courts."
elegant: Offenses against the state demand popular participation in judgment, since wronging the state wrongs every citizen. Such cases originate with and are finally decided by the people, though tried before agreed-upon magistrates. Private suits also require broad participation: exclusion from justice breeds alienation from the state. Every tribe has its own lottery-selected court for immediate decisions, with a final incorruptible court for unresolved disputes.
summary: Public offenses require popular participation in judgment since all citizens are wronged. Private suits also need broad civic involvement to prevent alienation. Each tribe has its own court with lottery-selected judges, and an incorruptible supreme court serves as final resort.
distilled: Participation in justice is participation in the state itself -- public offenses require popular judgment, every tribe needs its own court, and a supreme incorruptible court provides final resolution.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The principle that 'he who has no share in the administration of justice is apt to imagine that he has no share in the state at all' is a profound democratic insight, remarkable coming from Plato. It anticipates Aristotle's definition of citizenship as participation in judicial and deliberative functions (Politics III.1, 1275a22). The combination of popular participation (origination and final decision), elite trial (three magistrates), and lottery selection (tribal courts) reflects the Laws' characteristic blending of democratic, oligarchic, and meritocratic elements. Cf. Republic 557a on democratic participation and Crito 50a-51d on the citizen's relationship to law.

narrative:
"Thus much of the courts of law, which, as I was saying, cannot be precisely defined either as being or not being offices; a superficial sketch has been given of them, in which some things have been told and others omitted. For the right place of an exact statement of the laws respecting suits, under their several heads, will be at the end of the body of legislation; let us then expect them at the end.

Perfect unity and exactness, extending to the whole and every particular of political administration, cannot be attained to the full, until the discussion shall have a beginning, middle, and end, and is complete in every part. At present we have reached the election of magistrates, and this may be regarded as a sufficient termination of what preceded. And now there need no longer be any delay or hesitation in beginning the work of legislation."
refashioned:
"This is enough about courts of law for now -- which, as I noted, cannot be neatly classified as offices or non-offices. What I have given is a preliminary sketch, with some matters explained and others left out. The proper place for detailed laws governing lawsuits, organized by category, will be at the end of our legislative body; we should expect them there.

Perfect coherence and precision in political administration, covering both the whole and every particular, cannot be achieved until the entire discussion has a proper beginning, middle, and end, complete in all its parts. At this point we have finished with the election of magistrates, which serves as a satisfactory conclusion to everything that preceded it. Now we may begin the actual work of legislation without further delay or hesitation."
elegant: The treatment of courts remains a preliminary sketch -- detailed laws on litigation belong at the end of the legislative code. Perfect systematic coherence requires completing the whole before perfecting the parts. With magistrate elections established, the constitutional groundwork is laid. Now the substantive work of legislation can begin.
summary: The Athenian acknowledges that his treatment of courts is preliminary, with detailed litigation laws to come later. Having completed the constitutional groundwork of magistrate elections, he declares the state ready for substantive legislation.
distilled: With magistrate elections complete, the constitutional foundation is laid, and the substantive work of legislation can begin without further delay.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The methodological self-awareness here is characteristic of late Plato: the discussion must have 'beginning, middle, and end' before achieving full coherence -- echoing Aristotle's later prescription for dramatic unity (Poetics 1450b). The acknowledgment that courts 'cannot be precisely defined either as being or not being offices' shows philosophical honesty about classificatory difficulties. The transition from constitutional framework (offices, elections) to substantive legislation marks a major structural turning point in Book 6. Cf. Statesman 283c-285c on the importance of proper measure and completeness in political science.

narrative:
"I like what you have said, Stranger -- and I particularly like your manner of tacking on the beginning of your new discourse to the end of the former one."

"Thus far, then, the old men's rational pastime has gone off well."

"You mean, I suppose, their serious and noble pursuit?"

"Perhaps; but I should like to know whether you and I are agreed about a certain thing."

"About what thing?"

"You know the endless labour which painters expend upon their pictures -- they are always putting in or taking out colours, or whatever be the term which artists employ; they seem as if they would never cease touching up their works, which are always being made brighter and more beautiful."

"I know something of these matters from report, although I have never had any great acquaintance with the art."
refashioned:
"I like what you have said, Stranger, and I especially admire how you connected the beginning of your new topic to the conclusion of the previous one."

"So far, then, this rational pastime of ours -- old men reasoning together -- has gone rather well."

"You mean their serious and noble pursuit, I take it?"

"Perhaps. But I want to make sure you and I agree on a certain point."

"What point is that?"

"Consider the endless labor that painters devote to their pictures. They are constantly adding or removing colors -- or whatever term artists use for it. They seem never to stop refining their work, always making it brighter and more beautiful."

"I know something about that from what I have heard, though I have never been closely acquainted with the art myself."
elegant: Cleinias praises the Athenian's seamless transition between topics. The Athenian characterizes their dialogue as old men's rational pastime -- or, Cleinias corrects, a serious and noble pursuit. The Athenian then introduces an analogy: painters endlessly refine their work, always adding and adjusting to make it more beautiful.
summary: Cleinias compliments the Athenian's rhetorical transitions. The Athenian modestly calls their work a rational pastime, and Cleinias elevates it to a noble pursuit. The Athenian then introduces the analogy of painters who endlessly refine their work.
distilled: The dialogue is both rational pastime and noble pursuit, and legislation, like painting, requires endless refinement.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias (initiating), Athenian Stranger (responding). The painter analogy introduces a major theme: legislation as an ongoing work of art that requires perpetual refinement. The self-deprecating 'old men's rational pastime' (presbyton logike paidia) versus Cleinias's 'serious and noble pursuit' captures the Laws' characteristic tone -- philosophical seriousness leavened with ironic self-awareness about the limits of theoretical legislation. The painting metaphor will be extended in 6.38-39 to argue that laws need successors who can correct and improve them. Cf. Republic 472c-473b on the relationship between the theoretical 'painting' of the ideal state and its practical realization.

narrative:
"No matter; we may make use of the illustration notwithstanding: Suppose that some one had a mind to paint a figure in the most beautiful manner, in the hope that his work instead of losing would always improve as time went on -- do you not see that being a mortal, unless he leaves some one to succeed him who will correct the flaws which time may introduce, and be able to add what is left imperfect through the defect of the artist, and who will further brighten up and improve the picture, all his great labour will last but a short time?"

"True."
refashioned:
"No matter -- we can use the illustration regardless. Imagine a painter who creates the most beautiful figure he can, hoping that his work will not deteriorate but actually improve over time. Do you not see that, being mortal, unless he leaves behind a successor who can repair the flaws that time introduces, complete what the artist left imperfect, and continue to brighten and improve the painting, all his great labor will endure only briefly?"

"That is true."
elegant: Even the most beautiful painting will not endure unless the mortal artist leaves behind successors who can repair time's damage, complete imperfections, and continue improving the work. No human creation sustains itself without ongoing care.
summary: A painter's masterpiece will not last unless he leaves behind successors who can repair time's damage and continue improving the work. No mortal creation is self-sustaining.
distilled: Every mortal creation requires successors to maintain, repair, and improve it -- without them, even the finest work deteriorates.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The painting metaphor serves a practical political purpose: legislation, like art, requires ongoing stewardship. This passage prepares the argument (6.39-40) that guardians of the law must be trained not just to preserve but to improve the legal code. The mortality theme -- the artist is mortal, so the work needs successors -- adds urgency to institutional design. Cf. Republic 472d on the painter of the ideal state, and Statesman 293e-294a on the limitations of written law that cannot adapt to changing circumstances.

narrative: "And is not the aim of the legislator similar? First, he desires that his laws should be written down with all possible exactness; in the second place, as time goes on and he has made an actual trial of his decrees, will he not find omissions? Do you imagine that there ever was a legislator so foolish as not to know that many things are necessarily omitted, which some one coming after him must correct, if the constitution and the order of government is not to deteriorate, but to improve in the state which he has established?"
refashioned: "Is not the legislator's aim similar to the painter's? First, he wants his laws written down with the greatest possible precision. But second, as time passes and his decrees are tested in practice, will he not inevitably discover omissions? Can you imagine a legislator so foolish as to believe his code is complete -- not knowing that many things are necessarily left out, and that someone after him must correct these gaps if the constitution and political order are to improve rather than deteriorate?"
elegant: The legislator, like the painter, first aims for precision but inevitably discovers omissions through practical experience. No legislator can foresee everything; successors must correct gaps to prevent constitutional deterioration. Laws, like paintings, require ongoing revision.
summary: The legislator's situation parallels the painter's: laws are written with maximum precision but inevitably prove incomplete when tested in practice. Successors must correct these omissions to prevent constitutional decline.
distilled: No legislator's code is ever complete -- practical experience reveals inevitable omissions that successors must correct to prevent constitutional deterioration.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage marks a significant departure from the Republic's more static conception of the ideal state. The Laws acknowledges that legislation is inherently incomplete and requires ongoing revision -- a position closer to modern constitutional theory. The emphasis on 'actual trial of his decrees' reflects an empirical, pragmatic epistemology absent from the Republic's more rationalist approach. Cf. Statesman 294a-295b on the inherent limitations of written law, and Republic 425d-427a where Socrates dismisses the need for detailed legislation in the ideal state.

narrative:
"Assuredly, that is the sort of thing which every one would desire."

"And if any one possesses any means of accomplishing this by word or deed, or has any way great or small by which he can teach a person to understand how he can maintain and amend the laws, he should finish what he has to say, and not leave the work incomplete."

"By all means."

"And is not this what you and I have to do at the present moment?"

"What have we to do?"

"As we are about to legislate and have chosen our guardians of the law, and are ourselves in the evening of life, and they as compared with us are young men, we ought not only to legislate for them, but to endeavour to make them not only guardians of the law but legislators themselves, as far as this is possible."

"Certainly; if we can."

"At any rate, we must do our best."

"Of course."
refashioned:
"Certainly, every legislator would want that."

"And if anyone has any means -- through word or deed, great or small -- of teaching someone how to maintain and improve the laws, he must complete that teaching and not leave the work unfinished."

"Absolutely."

"And is that not precisely what you and I must do right now?"

"What do you mean?"

"We are about to legislate, and we have established guardians of the law. But we are in the evening of our lives, while the guardians are young by comparison. Therefore we must not merely legislate for them but strive to make them not just guardians of the law but legislators in their own right, to the greatest extent possible."

"Certainly, if we are able."

"At the very least, we must try our best."

"Of course."
elegant: The task is not merely to write laws but to train the guardians of the law to become legislators themselves. Since the founders are old and the guardians young, the transmission of legislative wisdom -- the capacity not just to enforce but to maintain and improve the legal code -- is essential. Teaching successors to be co-creators of the law is the highest duty of the departing generation.
summary: The interlocutors agree that their task extends beyond writing laws: they must train the young guardians of the law to become legislators themselves, transmitting the capacity to maintain and improve the legal code across generations.
distilled: The founders' highest duty is training guardians of the law to become legislators in their own right, ensuring the code can be maintained and improved after the founders are gone.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias (responding), Athenian Stranger (primary). The 'evening of life' metaphor poignantly underscores the intergenerational nature of political founding. The transformation of guardians from mere enforcers into legislators represents the Laws' highest ambition for institutional continuity. This parallels Republic 502c-503a on the philosopher-king's role in training successors, but the Laws' approach is more practical: training within existing legal frameworks rather than through philosophical dialectic. The insistence on completing the teaching -- 'not leave the work incomplete' -- reflects the passage's theme of perpetual institutional refinement.

narrative:
"We will say to them -- O friends and saviours of our laws, in laying down any law, there are many particulars which we shall omit, and this cannot be helped; at the same time, we will do our utmost to describe what is important, and will give an outline which you shall fill up. And I will explain on what principle you are to act.

Megillus and Cleinias and I have often spoken to one another touching these matters, and we are of opinion that we have spoken well. And we hope that you will be of the same mind with us, and become our disciples, and keep in view the things which in our united opinion the legislator and guardian of the law ought to keep in view.

There was one main point about which we were agreed -- that a man's whole energies throughout life should be devoted to the acquisition of the virtue proper to a man, whether this was to be gained by study, or habit, or some mode of acquisition, or desire, or opinion, or knowledge -- and this applies equally to men and women, old and young -- the aim of all should always be such as I have described; anything which may be an impediment, the good man ought to show that he utterly disregards.

And if at last necessity plainly compels him to be an outlaw from his native land, rather than bow his neck to the yoke of slavery and be ruled by inferiors, and he has to fly, an exile he must be and endure all such trials, rather than accept another form of government, which is likely to make men worse.

These are our original principles; and do you now, fixing your eyes upon the standard of what a man and a citizen ought or ought not to be, praise and blame the laws -- blame those which have not this power of making the citizen better, but embrace those which have; and with gladness receive and live in them; bidding a long farewell to other institutions which aim at goods, as they are termed, of a different kind."
refashioned:
"We will address our guardians thus: 'Friends and protectors of our laws, in drafting any law there will be many details we must inevitably omit. Nevertheless, we will do our best to describe what matters most, providing an outline for you to complete. Let me explain the principle by which you should work.

Megillus, Cleinias, and I have discussed these matters extensively, and we believe we have reached sound conclusions. We hope you will share our views and become our students, keeping always in mind what we believe the legislator and guardian of the law should focus on.

Our central agreement was this: a person's entire energy throughout life should be devoted to acquiring virtue -- whether through study, habit, practice, desire, conviction, or knowledge. This applies equally to men and women, old and young. The pursuit of virtue should be everyone's supreme aim, and a good person should show utter contempt for anything that obstructs it.

If necessity ultimately forces a person into exile from his homeland rather than submit to slavery under inferior rulers, then exile he must accept and all its hardships, rather than live under a government that makes people worse.

These are our founding principles. Keep your eyes fixed on the standard of what a person and citizen should be. Praise laws that have the power to make citizens better; condemn those that do not. Embrace the good laws joyfully and live by them, bidding permanent farewell to institutions that pursue so-called goods of a different and lesser kind.'"
elegant: The address to future guardians: the founders' code is inevitably incomplete, requiring the guardians to fill in the outline. The governing principle is singular -- all human energy must be devoted to acquiring virtue, equally for men and women, old and young. Nothing may obstruct this aim; exile is preferable to servitude under a regime that degrades character. Laws should be judged by one standard alone: do they make citizens better?
summary: The Athenian instructs future guardians of the law to fill the gaps in legislation according to one supreme principle: all human energy must pursue virtue, equally for men and women. Exile is preferable to living under a government that corrupts character. Laws should be judged solely by whether they make citizens better.
distilled: The single standard for all legislation is whether it promotes virtue -- exile is preferable to submission to a government that makes people worse.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage articulates the Laws' moral foundation in its most concentrated form. The primacy of virtue echoes Republic 352d-354a (justice as the soul's proper function) and the Apology (38a: the unexamined life is not worth living). The gender equality in the pursuit of virtue ('men and women, old and young') is notable and parallels Republic 451c-457b. The exile-over-degradation principle echoes Socrates' refusal to compromise in the Apology and Crito. The instruction to 'fill up' the legislative outline transforms guardians from passive enforcers into active co-legislators -- the culmination of 6.38-40's painter analogy. Cf. Statesman 309c-311c on the true statesman's commitment to virtue above all.

narrative:
"Let us proceed to another class of laws, beginning with their foundation in religion. And we must first return to the number 5040 -- the entire number had, and has, a great many convenient divisions, and the number of the tribes which was a twelfth part of the whole, being correctly formed by 21 x 20 (5040/(21 x 20), i.e., 5040/420=12), also has them. And not only is the whole number divisible by twelve, but also the number of each tribe is divisible by twelve.

Now every portion should be regarded by us as a sacred gift of Heaven, corresponding to the months and to the revolution of the universe. Every city has a guiding and sacred principle given by nature, but in some the division or distribution has been more right than in others, and has been more sacred and fortunate. In our opinion, nothing can be more right than the selection of the number 5040, which may be divided by all numbers from one to twelve with the single exception of eleven, and that admits of a very easy correction; for if, turning to the dividend (5040), we deduct two families, the defect in the division is cured. And the truth of this may be easily proved when we have leisure.

But for the present, trusting to the mere assertion of this principle, let us divide the state; and assigning to each portion some God or son of a God, let us give them altars and sacred rites, and at the altars let us hold assemblies for sacrifice twice in the month -- twelve assemblies for the tribes, and twelve for the city, according to their divisions; the first in honour of the Gods and divine things, and the second to promote friendship and 'better acquaintance,' as the phrase is, and every sort of good fellowship with one another.

For people must be acquainted with those into whose families and whom they marry and with those to whom they give in marriage; in such matters, as far as possible, a man should deem it all important to avoid a mistake, and with this serious purpose let games be instituted in which youths and maidens shall dance together, seeing one another and being seen naked, at a proper age, and on a suitable occasion, not transgressing the rules of modesty."
refashioned:
"Let us turn to another category of laws, beginning with their religious foundations. We must first revisit the number 5040, which has an extraordinary number of convenient divisions. The twelve tribes, each being a twelfth of the whole, are correctly formed since 5040 divided by 420 (21 times 20) equals 12. Both the total and each tribal portion are divisible by twelve.

Every numerical portion should be regarded as a sacred gift from Heaven, corresponding to the months and the cosmic cycle. Every city has a guiding sacred principle given by nature, though some cities achieve more fortunate divisions than others. In our view, nothing surpasses the number 5040, which is divisible by every number from one to twelve except eleven -- and that deficiency is easily corrected by subtracting two households. This can be proved at leisure.

For now, accepting this principle, let us divide the state and assign each portion to a god or divine being, establishing altars and sacred rites. At these altars, sacrificial assemblies shall be held twice monthly -- twelve for the tribes and twelve for the city. The first assembly honors the gods and divine things; the second promotes friendship, mutual acquaintance, and fellowship among citizens.

People must know the families into which they marry and to whom they give their children in marriage. Mistakes in such matters must be avoided at all costs. For this serious purpose, festivals with games shall be instituted where young men and women dance together, seeing one another and being seen in natural form, at a proper age and appropriate occasion, without transgressing the rules of modesty."
elegant: The number 5040 is nearly perfect -- divisible by every integer from 1 to 12 except 11, a defect easily corrected. Each numerical portion is a sacred gift mirroring the cosmic order. The state is divided under divine patronage, with twice-monthly assemblies: one for worship, one for social fellowship. Marriage requires mutual knowledge, so festivals with modest but natural displays allow young people to evaluate potential partners. Religion, mathematics, and social life converge in institutional design.
summary: The number 5040, with its extraordinary divisibility, grounds the state's sacred-mathematical order. Each tribal portion has a patron deity with regular assemblies for worship and fellowship. Marriage festivals where youth see each other dancing promote informed mate selection within modesty's bounds.
distilled: The state's sacred order rests on the mathematically perfect number 5040, with twice-monthly assemblies for worship and social fellowship, and festivals for marriage-oriented acquaintance among the young.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The number 5040 (= 7!) is central to the Laws' mathematical-theological framework. Its extraordinary divisibility (59 factors) enables flexible administrative subdivision. The cosmic correspondence -- portions matching months and cosmic revolution -- reflects Pythagorean influence and the Laws' vision of the state as a microcosm. The marriage festivals with modest nudity parallel Republic 452a-e on gymnastic nudity for both sexes.
