The dual purpose of assemblies (worship + social bonding) reflects the Laws' integration of religion and civic life. The mathematical-theological foundation here contrasts with the Republic's reliance on the Form of the Good (Republic 509b) -- the Laws grounds political order in number and divine patronage rather than metaphysical abstraction.

narrative:
"The directors of choruses will be the superintendents and regulators of these games, and they, together with the guardians of the law, will legislate in any matters which we have omitted; for, as we said, where there are numerous and minute details, the legislator must leave out something.

And the annual officers who have experience, and know what is wanted, must make arrangements and improvements year by year, until such enactments and provisions are sufficiently determined. A ten years' experience of sacrifices and dances, if extending to all particulars, will be quite sufficient; and if the legislator be alive they shall communicate with him, but if he be dead then the several officers shall refer the omissions which come under their notice to the guardians of the law, and correct them, until all is perfect; and from that time there shall be no more change, and they shall establish and use the new laws with the others which the legislator originally gave them, and of which they are never, if they can help, to change aught; or, if some necessity overtakes them, the magistrates must be called into counsel, and the whole people, and they must go to all the oracles of the Gods; and if they are all agreed, in that case they may make the change, but if they are not agreed, by no manner of means, and any one who dissents shall prevail, as the law ordains."
refashioned:
"The chorus directors shall supervise and regulate these games. Together with the guardians of the law, they shall legislate on matters we have omitted -- for as we acknowledged, the legislator inevitably leaves out numerous small details.

Annual officers with practical experience shall make adjustments and improvements year by year until these provisions are thoroughly refined. Ten years of experience with sacrifices and dances, attending to every detail, should be quite sufficient. If the original legislator is still alive, they shall consult him. If he has died, the officers shall report any gaps they discover to the guardians of the law, who shall correct them until the code is perfected.

From that point forward, no further changes shall be made. The refined laws shall stand alongside the original legislation, which should never be altered if it can be helped. Should genuine necessity require a change, all magistrates must be consulted, along with the entire populace, and all the oracles of the gods must be sought. Only if everyone agrees may the change be made. If there is any dissent whatsoever, the proposed change shall not proceed -- the dissenting voice prevails, as the law ordains."
elegant: Chorus directors and guardians of the law fill legislative gaps through ten years of practical refinement, consulting the original legislator if alive. After this period, the code is fixed. Future changes require unanimous agreement of magistrates, the people, and the divine oracles. Any single dissent blocks change. The law favors stability over innovation, with an extraordinarily high bar for amendment.
summary: Chorus directors and guardians fill legislative gaps over a ten-year refinement period. After that, the legal code becomes fixed. Any future change requires unanimous consent of magistrates, the people, and divine oracles -- a single dissent blocks amendment.
distilled: After ten years of practical refinement, the legal code becomes fixed, with future amendments requiring unanimous agreement of magistrates, people, and oracles.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The ten-year refinement period reflects a pragmatic approach: laws are tested and adjusted through experience before becoming permanent. The extremely conservative amendment process -- requiring unanimity of magistrates, people, and oracles, with any dissent blocking change -- creates an almost unalterable legal code. This reflects Plato's deep suspicion of innovation (kainotomia) in political matters, expressed also in Republic 424b-c (no changes in music or gymnastics). The requirement to consult 'all the oracles' elevates divine authority above human political will. Cf. Crito 50a-54d on the authority of the laws and the danger of individual citizens undermining them.

narrative:
"Whenever any one over twenty-five years of age, having seen and been seen by others, believes himself to have found a marriage connection which is to his mind, and suitable for the procreation of children, let him marry if he be still under the age of five-and-thirty years; but let him first hear how he ought to seek after what is suitable and appropriate. For, as Cleinias says, every law should have a suitable prelude."

"You recollect at the right moment, Stranger, and do not miss the opportunity which the argument affords of saying a word in season."
refashioned:
"When any man over twenty-five, having observed others and been observed by them in return, believes he has found a suitable marriage partner for the procreation of children, let him marry, provided he is still under thirty-five. But first he should hear guidance on how to seek what is truly suitable and appropriate. For as Cleinias reminds us, every law deserves a fitting prelude."

"You recall that at just the right moment, Stranger, and you take the opportunity the argument offers to say exactly what is needed."
elegant: Men between twenty-five and thirty-five who have found a suitable partner through mutual acquaintance should marry. But before the marriage law itself, a prelude is needed -- guidance on what makes a marriage truly suitable. Every law deserves its proper introduction.
summary: Marriage should occur between ages twenty-five and thirty-five, after a period of mutual observation. Before stating the marriage law, the Athenian announces a prelude on what constitutes a suitable match, following the principle that every law deserves a fitting introduction.
distilled: Marriage between twenty-five and thirty-five follows a period of mutual acquaintance, and the law requires a prelude explaining what makes a match truly suitable.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The marriage age range (25-35 for men) reflects Greek norms, though the lower bound is later than typical Athenian practice (where men often married around 30, women around 15). The 'prelude' (prooimion) concept is one of the Laws' distinctive innovations -- the idea that laws should persuade as well as command, with philosophical justifications preceding prescriptions. This was introduced in Laws Book 4 (722d-723d). Cleinias's praise of the prelude concept reflects his growing philosophical sophistication. The transition from festivals and acquaintance (6.42) to marriage proper shows careful legislative sequencing.

narrative:
"I thank you. We will say to him who is born of good parents -- O my son, you ought to make such a marriage as wise men would approve. Now they would advise you neither to avoid a poor marriage, nor specially to desire a rich one; but if other things are equal, always to honour inferiors, and with them to form connections -- this will be for the benefit of the city and of the families which are united; for the equable and symmetrical tends infinitely more to virtue than the unmixed.

And he who is conscious of being too headstrong, and carried away more than is fitting in all his actions, ought to desire to become the relation of orderly parents; and he who is of the opposite temper ought to seek the opposite alliance. Let there be one word concerning all marriages: Every man shall follow, not after the marriage which is most pleasing to himself, but after that which is most beneficial to the state.

For somehow every one is by nature prone to that which is likest to himself, and in this way the whole city becomes unequal in property and in disposition; and hence there arise in most states the very results which we least desire to happen.

Now, to add to the law an express provision, not only that the rich man shall not marry into the rich family, nor the powerful into the family of the powerful, but that the slower natures shall be compelled to enter into marriage with the quicker, and the quicker with the slower, may awaken anger as well as laughter in the minds of many; for there is a difficulty in perceiving that the city ought to be well mingled like a cup, in which the maddening wine is hot and fiery, but when chastened by a soberer God, receives a fair associate and becomes an excellent and temperate drink.

Yet in marriage no one is able to see that the same result occurs. Wherefore also the law must let alone such matters, but we should try to charm the spirits of men into believing the equability of their children's disposition to be of more importance than equality in excessive fortune when they marry; and him who is too desirous of making a rich marriage we should endeavour to turn aside by reproaches, not, however, by any compulsion of written law."
refashioned:
"Thank you. The prelude to marriage law shall be this: 'Son of good parents, you should make the kind of marriage that wise people would approve. They would advise you neither to shun a match with someone of modest means nor to actively pursue wealth. All else being equal, you should always prefer to marry into a family of lower standing -- this benefits both the city and the united families, since balanced and blended unions tend far more toward virtue than unmixed ones.

If you know yourself to be headstrong and impulsive in your conduct, you should seek marriage with an orderly, temperate family. If you are naturally reserved and slow, seek the opposite. One principle governs all marriages: every man should pursue not the marriage most pleasing to himself but the one most beneficial to the state.

Human nature inclines everyone toward what resembles themselves, and this tendency creates ever-widening inequalities in both wealth and temperament across the city -- producing precisely the outcomes we most wish to avoid.

Now, to legislate explicitly that the rich must not marry the rich, the powerful not the powerful, and that slow temperaments must be legally compelled to marry quick ones and vice versa -- this would provoke both outrage and ridicule in many people. It is difficult to see that a city, like a cup of wine, must be properly blended: the hot, fiery wine, tempered by a soberer element, becomes an excellent and balanced drink.

Yet when it comes to marriage, people cannot perceive this same principle at work. Therefore the law should not compel such mixing directly. Instead, we should use persuasion to charm people into valuing balanced temperament in their children above accumulation of excessive wealth through marriage. Those too eager for rich marriages should be turned aside through social disapproval, not through legislative force.'"
elegant: The marriage prelude: marry for the city's benefit, not personal preference. Prefer matches with families of lower station, since blended unions produce greater virtue than concentrated ones. The headstrong should marry the temperate, and vice versa. Like wine that needs a sobering element to become an excellent drink, the city needs mixed marriages to prevent the concentration of wealth and character extremes. But this mixing should be encouraged through persuasion and social pressure, not compelled by law.
summary: The marriage prelude advises choosing partners for the city's benefit over personal preference, favoring marriages that blend opposite temperaments and different wealth levels. Like wine needing a sobering element, the city needs mixed marriages. However, this should be achieved through persuasion rather than legal compulsion.
distilled: Marriage should blend opposite temperaments and wealth levels for the city's benefit -- like wine needing a tempering element -- but through persuasion rather than legal force.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The wine-mixing metaphor (krasis) is one of Plato's most vivid images for political moderation, recalling Statesman 306a-311c on the weaving together of opposite temperaments. The distinction between persuasion and compulsion in marriage law exemplifies the Laws' prelude doctrine: where direct legislation would provoke resistance, the legislator uses rhetoric and social norms. This contrasts sharply with Republic 458c-461e, where mating is directly controlled by rulers through a 'noble lie.' The Laws' approach is more realistic and less authoritarian. The principle that like marries like, producing civic inequality, anticipates modern concerns about assortative mating.

narrative: "Let this then be our exhortation concerning marriage, and let us remember what was said before -- that a man should cling to immortality, and leave behind him children's children to be the servants of God in his place for ever. All this and much more may be truly said by way of prelude about the duty of marriage."
refashioned: "Let this be our exhortation regarding marriage. And let us recall what was said earlier: that a person should grasp at immortality by leaving behind children and grandchildren who will serve God in his place forever. All this, and much more besides, can be truly said as a prelude about the duty of marriage."
elegant: Marriage is a duty through which humans grasp at immortality, leaving behind generations who continue to serve the divine. This is the essence of the marriage prelude.
summary: The marriage prelude concludes with the reminder that marriage is a sacred duty through which humans achieve a kind of immortality, leaving behind descendants who serve God in perpetuity.
distilled: Marriage is the means by which mortals achieve immortality, leaving behind descendants who serve God in their place forever.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The concept of biological immortality through offspring connects to Symposium 207a-208b, where Diotima explains that mortal beings participate in immortality through reproduction. The phrase 'servants of God' elevates procreation from a biological to a theological duty -- children continue their parents' divine service. This theological framing of marriage as duty is characteristic of the Laws' religious orientation, absent from the Republic's more philosophical treatment of mating (Republic 458c-461e). The brevity of this passage signals it as a closing summary of the longer prelude in 6.45.

narrative:
"But if a man will not listen and remains unsocial and alien among his fellow-citizens, and is still unmarried at thirty-five years of age, let him pay a yearly fine -- he who of the highest class shall pay a fine of a hundred drachmae, and he who is of the second class a fine of seventy drachmae; the third class shall pay sixty drachmae, and the fourth thirty drachmae, and let the money be sacred to Hera; he who does not pay the fine annually shall owe ten times the sum, which the treasurer of the goddess shall exact; and if he fails in doing so, let him be answerable and give an account of the money at his audit.

He who refuses to marry shall be thus punished in money, and also be deprived of all honour which the younger show to the elder; let no young man voluntarily obey him, and if he attempt to punish any one, let every one come to the rescue and defend the injured person, and he who is present and does not come to the rescue, shall be pronounced by the law to be a coward and a bad citizen.

Of the marriage portion I have already spoken; and again I say for the instruction of poor men that he who neither gives nor receives a dowry on account of poverty, has a compensation; for the citizens of our state are provided with the necessaries of life, and wives will be less likely to be insolent, and husbands to be mean and subservient to them on account of property.

And he who obeys this law will do a noble action; but he who will not obey, and gives or receives more than fifty drachmae as the price of the marriage garments if he be of the lowest, or more than a mina, or a mina and-a-half, if he be of the third or second classes, or two minae if he be of the highest class, shall owe to the public treasury a similar sum, and that which is given or received shall be sacred to Hera and Zeus; and let the treasurers of these Gods exact the money, as was said before about the unmarried -- that the treasurers of Hera were to exact the money, or pay the fine themselves."
refashioned:
"But if a man refuses to listen and remains unsocial and alienated from his fellow citizens, still unmarried at thirty-five, he shall pay an annual fine: one hundred drachmae for the highest property class, seventy for the second, sixty for the third, and thirty for the fourth. This money shall be sacred to Hera. Anyone who fails to pay annually shall owe ten times the amount, to be collected by Hera's treasurer; if the treasurer fails to collect, he must account for the shortfall at his audit.

The man who refuses to marry shall suffer not only monetary penalties but also social consequences: he shall lose all the respect that younger citizens normally show to their elders. No young person shall voluntarily obey him. If such a man tries to discipline anyone, everyone present must come to the other person's defense. Anyone who witnesses this and fails to intervene shall be legally declared a coward and bad citizen.

Regarding dowries, I have spoken before. Let me restate for the benefit of those with modest means: a man who neither gives nor receives a dowry because of poverty suffers no real loss, since our state provides all citizens with life's necessities. Moreover, without large dowries, wives are less likely to become arrogant and husbands less likely to become servile over money matters.

Obeying this dowry law is a noble act. But anyone who disobeys -- giving or receiving more than fifty drachmae for marriage garments if he is of the lowest class, or more than a mina to a mina and a half for the middle classes, or more than two minae for the highest class -- shall owe the public treasury an equal sum. The excess dowry shall be dedicated to Hera and Zeus, and their treasurers shall collect it, or face the consequences themselves."
elegant: Bachelors at thirty-five face graduated fines sacred to Hera, with penalties multiplied tenfold for non-payment. Beyond money, the unmarried man loses all social authority: the young need not respect or obey him, and anyone who defends him against this treatment is declared a bad citizen. Dowries are capped by class to prevent wealth from distorting marriages -- the state provides necessities, making large dowries unnecessary and their social effects (wifely arrogance, husbandly servility) undesirable.
summary: Men unmarried at thirty-five face graduated fines and loss of social authority -- the young need not respect or obey them. Dowries are capped by property class, since the state provides necessities and large dowries distort marital relationships through arrogance and servility.
distilled: Bachelors face escalating fines and complete loss of social authority, while dowry caps prevent wealth from corrupting marriage relationships.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The penalties for bachelorhood are exceptionally severe: not just fines but total loss of social standing, including the right to discipline the young. This reflects the Laws' view of marriage as a civic duty, not a personal choice. The dedication of fines to Hera (goddess of marriage) gives enforcement a sacred dimension. The dowry caps are remarkably egalitarian, explicitly designed to prevent wealth-based power dynamics within marriage. The observation that large dowries make 'wives insolent and husbands mean and subservient' shows psychological insight into how money distorts intimate relationships. Cf. Republic 464b-e on the abolition of private property and family among guardians -- the Laws preserves marriage and property but regulates both carefully.

narrative:
"The betrothal by a father shall be valid in the first degree, that by a grandfather in the second degree, and in the third degree, betrothal by brothers who have the same father; but if there are none of these alive, the betrothal by a mother shall be valid in like manner; in cases of unexampled fatality, the next of kin and the guardians shall have authority.

What are to be the rites before marriages, or any other sacred acts, relating either to future, present, or past marriages, shall be referred to the interpreters; and he who follows their advice may be satisfied.

Touching the marriage festival, they shall assemble not more than five male and five female friends of both families; and a like number of members of the family of either sex, and no man shall spend more than his means will allow; he who is of the richest class may spend a mina -- he who is of the second, half a mina, and in the same proportion as the census of each decreases: all men shall praise him who is obedient to the law; but he who is disobedient shall be punished by the guardians of the law as a man wanting in true taste, and uninstructed in the laws of bridal song.

Drunkenness is always improper, except at the festivals of the God who gave wine; and peculiarly dangerous, when a man is engaged in the business of marriage; at such a crisis of their lives a bride and bridegroom ought to have all their wits about them -- they ought to take care that their offspring may be born of reasonable beings; for on what day or night Heaven will give them increase, who can say? Moreover, they ought not to be begetting children when their bodies are dissipated by intoxication, but their offspring should be compact and solid, quiet and compounded properly; whereas the drunkard is all abroad in all his actions, and beside himself both in body and soul."
refashioned:
"Betrothal authority follows this hierarchy: first the father, then the grandfather, then brothers sharing the same father. If none of these survive, the mother's betrothal is equally valid. In cases of extraordinary misfortune where even she is absent, the next of kin and the guardians shall have authority.

All religious rites pertaining to marriages -- whether future, present, or past -- shall be determined by the interpreters. Anyone who follows their guidance may rest assured.

Regarding the wedding celebration: no more than five male and five female friends from each family shall attend, along with an equal number of family members of each sex. Spending must not exceed one's means: the richest class may spend up to one mina, the second class half a mina, and so on in proportion to each census bracket. Society shall praise those who observe this law, and the guardians of the law shall punish the disobedient as persons of poor taste who are ignorant of proper wedding tradition.

Drunkenness is always improper except at festivals of the god who gave wine, and it is especially dangerous during marriage. At this critical moment in their lives, bride and bridegroom must have their full faculties. They should ensure that their offspring are born of sober, reasonable parents -- for who can predict on which day or night Heaven will grant them a child? Children should not be conceived when the parents' bodies are corrupted by intoxication. Offspring should be sound, stable, and properly formed, whereas the drunkard is unstable in every action, beside himself in both body and soul."
elegant: Betrothal authority descends through father, grandfather, brothers, then mother, with kinsmen and guardians as last resort. Wedding rites follow the interpreters' guidance. Wedding celebrations are limited in size and spending proportional to class. Drunkenness at weddings is especially condemned: children should be conceived by sober, clear-headed parents, since intoxication produces unstable offspring. The drunkard is disordered in body and soul alike.
summary: Betrothal authority follows a family hierarchy from father through kinsmen. Weddings are capped in both attendance and spending by class. Drunkenness at weddings is condemned because children should be conceived by sober parents -- intoxication produces disordered offspring.
distilled: Betrothal follows family hierarchy, weddings are regulated in size and cost, and sobriety at marriage is essential because conception by intoxicated parents produces disordered children.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The betrothal hierarchy reflects standard Greek patriarchal practice, with the notable inclusion of the mother's authority in the absence of male relatives. The spending limits reinforce the anti-luxury ethic pervading the Laws' marriage legislation. The concern with sobriety at conception reflects Greek beliefs about prenatal influence (cf. Aristotle, Politics VII.16, 1335b) and the Laws' broader project of regulating private life for civic benefit. The exception for festivals of Dionysus acknowledges the god of wine's domain while restricting intoxication elsewhere. Cf. Laws Book 1's discussion of the proper use of wine (637a-650b).

narrative:
"Wherefore, also, the drunken man is bad and unsteady in sowing the seed of increase, and is likely to beget offspring who will be unstable and untrustworthy, and cannot be expected to walk straight either in body or mind. Hence during the whole year and all his life long, and especially while he is begetting children, he ought to take care and not intentionally do what is injurious to health, or what involves insolence and wrong; for he cannot help leaving the impression of himself on the souls and bodies of his offspring, and he begets children in every way inferior.

And especially on the day and night of marriage should a man abstain from such things. For the beginning, which is also a God dwelling in man, preserves all things, if it meet with proper respect from each individual.

He who marries is further to consider that one of the two houses in the lot is the nest and nursery of his young, and there he is to marry and make a home for himself and bring up his children, going away from his father and mother. For in friendships there must be some degree of desire, in order to cement and bind together diversities of character; but excessive intercourse not having the desire which is created by time, insensibly dissolves friendships from a feeling of satiety; wherefore a man and his wife shall leave to his and her father and mother their own dwelling-places, and themselves go as to a colony and dwell there, and visit and be visited by their parents; and they shall beget and bring up children, handing on the torch of life from one generation to another, and worshipping the Gods according to law for ever."
refashioned:
"A drunkard is unreliable in the act of procreation and will likely produce offspring who are unstable and untrustworthy, unable to go straight in either body or mind. Therefore, throughout the entire year and indeed his whole life -- but especially during the period of begetting children -- a man must take care not to do anything intentionally harmful to health or involving reckless behavior. He inevitably stamps his own image on his children's souls and bodies, producing inferior offspring through his own vice.

Above all, on the wedding day and night itself, a man must abstain from such conduct. The beginning is itself a god dwelling within human beings, and it preserves all things when given proper reverence.

The married man should also understand that one of the two houses in his land allotment is to serve as the nest and nursery for his children. There he shall establish his household and raise his family, having left his father's and mother's home. In friendships, some degree of longing is necessary to bind together different characters; but excessive proximity, lacking the desire that only absence creates, gradually dissolves affection through satiety. Therefore husband and wife shall leave their parents' dwellings and go to their own home as if to a new colony. They shall visit their parents and be visited in return, begetting and raising children, passing the torch of life from generation to generation, and worshipping the gods according to law forever."
elegant: The drunkard stamps his disorder on his children's bodies and souls. The beginning is sacred -- a god dwelling in human beings -- and must be treated with reverence. Newlyweds should establish their own household apart from parents: friendship requires the desire that distance creates, while excessive proximity breeds satiety. Marriage is a new colony where the couple passes the torch of life across generations in perpetual divine worship.
summary: Parental conduct directly imprints upon offspring, making sobriety especially crucial at conception. The beginning is sacred. Newlyweds must establish independent households because distance preserves desire while proximity breeds satiety. Marriage is a new colony where the torch of life passes through generations.
distilled: The beginning is a god in man -- parental conduct stamps children's character, and newlyweds must establish their own household to preserve affection through healthy distance from parents.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The declaration that 'the beginning is also a God dwelling in man' (arche kai theos en anthropois) is one of the Laws' most metaphysically rich statements, elevating the moment of conception to a divine event. The insight about distance and desire in relationships is psychologically acute: excessive proximity breeds satiety while moderate separation sustains longing. The 'torch of life' (lampas) metaphor evokes the Greek torch-relay race (lampadephoria) and transforms biological reproduction into a sacred relay. The instruction to leave parents echoes the Genesis injunction (2:24), though independently derived. Cf. Symposium 206c-207a on procreation as the mortal's participation in immortality.

narrative:
"In the next place, we have to consider what sort of property will be most convenient. There is no difficulty either in understanding or acquiring most kinds of property, but there is great difficulty in what relates to slaves. And the reason is that we speak about them in a way which is right and which is not right; for what we say about our slaves is consistent and also inconsistent with our practice about them."

"I do not understand, Stranger, what you mean."
refashioned:
"Next, we must consider what kinds of property will be most suitable. There is no great difficulty in understanding or acquiring most forms of property, but the matter of slaves presents serious problems. The reason is that we speak about slaves in ways that are simultaneously right and wrong -- our statements about them are both consistent and inconsistent with how we actually treat them."

"I do not understand what you mean, Stranger."
elegant: Most property is straightforward, but slavery presents a unique difficulty: what we say about slaves is both right and wrong, consistent and inconsistent with our actual treatment of them. The contradiction between theory and practice in slavery creates a genuine philosophical problem.
summary: The Athenian transitions to property, noting that most types are straightforward. Slavery, however, presents a unique difficulty: our discourse about slaves is simultaneously consistent and inconsistent with how we treat them.
distilled: Slavery presents a unique philosophical difficulty because our words about slaves are simultaneously consistent and inconsistent with our actual practice.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Megillus (responding). This is a remarkable moment of Platonic honesty: the Athenian acknowledges that the institution of slavery generates irresolvable contradictions between theory and practice. Megillus's confusion is genuine -- a Spartan would not easily see the problem, since Sparta's helot system was straightforwardly coercive without pretense of mutual benefit. The passage anticipates the fuller treatment in 6.51-53. Cf. Republic 469b-c on not enslaving Greeks, and Aristotle's defense of 'natural slavery' in Politics I.4-7, which attempts to resolve the very contradiction Plato here identifies.

narrative:
"I am not surprised, Megillus, for the state of the Helots among the Lacedaemonians is of all Hellenic forms of slavery the most controverted and disputed about, some approving and some condemning it; there is less dispute about the slavery which exists among the Heracleots, who have subjugated the Mariandynians, and about the Thessalian Penestae.

Looking at these and the like examples, what ought we to do concerning property in slaves? I made a remark, in passing, which naturally elicited a question about my meaning from you. It was this: We know that all would agree that we should have the best and most attached slaves whom we can get. For many a man has found his slaves better in every way than brethren or sons, and many times they have saved the lives and property of their masters and their whole house -- such tales are well known."
refashioned:
"I am not surprised by your confusion, Megillus. The Helot system among the Spartans is the most hotly debated form of slavery in all of Greece -- some defend it and others condemn it. There is less controversy about the Heracleots' subjugation of the Mariandynians, or the Thessalian system of Penestae.

Considering these and similar examples, what should we do about property in slaves? The remark I made in passing, which naturally prompted your question, was this: everyone would agree that we should try to have the best and most loyal slaves possible. History is full of cases where slaves have proven better in every way than brothers or sons, saving their masters' lives, property, and entire households. Such stories are well known."
elegant: The Helot system is Greece's most contested form of slavery. Despite the institution's contradictions, all agree that the best slaves are loyal ones, and history records many slaves who surpassed brothers and sons in devotion, saving lives and households. The question remains: how should the colony approach slavery?
summary: The Athenian acknowledges that Sparta's Helot system is Greece's most controversial institution. Despite slavery's contradictions, everyone agrees that loyal slaves are desirable, and many slaves have proven more faithful than family members.
distilled: Sparta's Helot system is Greece's most debated form of slavery, yet everyone agrees that loyal slaves have often surpassed even brothers and sons in devotion.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The reference to three different forms of Greek servitude -- Spartan Helots, Heracleot subjugation of Mariandynians, Thessalian Penestae -- shows Plato's awareness that 'slavery' encompassed diverse institutional forms. The Helot system was uniquely problematic because Helots were Greeks enslaving Greeks, maintaining their communal identity and posing constant revolt threats (cf. the Messenian revolts). The acknowledgment that slaves can surpass family members in loyalty complicates any simple theory of natural inferiority. Cf. Aristotle, Politics I.6 (1255b) on slaves who are 'naturally' slavish, a position the Laws refuses to adopt so cleanly.

narrative:
"To be sure."

"But may we not also say that the soul of the slave is utterly corrupt, and that no man of sense ought to trust them? And the wisest of our poets, speaking of Zeus, says:

'Far-seeing Zeus takes away half the understanding of men whom the day of slavery subdues.'

Different persons have got these two different notions of slaves in their minds -- some of them utterly distrust their servants, and, as if they were wild beasts, chastise them with goads and whips, and make their souls three times, or rather many times, as slavish as they were before; and others do just the opposite."

"True."

"Then what are we to do in our own country, Stranger, seeing that there are such differences in the treatment of slaves by their owners?"

"Well, Cleinias, there can be no doubt that man is a troublesome animal, and therefore he is not very manageable, nor likely to become so, when you attempt to introduce the necessary division, slave, and freeman, and master."

"That is obvious."
refashioned:
"Certainly."

"But may we not also say the opposite -- that the soul of a slave is utterly corrupt, and that no sensible person should trust them? The wisest of our poets, speaking of Zeus, says:

'Far-seeing Zeus takes away half the understanding of men whom the day of slavery subdues.'

People hold these two contradictory views of slaves simultaneously. Some utterly distrust their servants and, treating them like wild animals, discipline them with goads and whips, making their souls three times -- or rather many times -- more slavish than before. Others take the completely opposite approach."

"True."

"So what should we do in our own state, Stranger, given these wildly different approaches to managing slaves?"

"Well, Cleinias, there is no question that human beings are troublesome creatures, not easily managed. The difficulty only increases when you try to impose the necessary distinctions of slave, free person, and master."

"That much is obvious."
elegant: Two contradictory views of slaves exist simultaneously: slaves can be supremely loyal, yet Homer tells us Zeus takes half the mind of those whom slavery subdues. Some masters treat slaves as wild beasts, making them more slavish through brutality; others do the opposite. The fundamental problem is that human beings are difficult to manage, and the division into slave, free, and master makes them only more so.
summary: Two contradictory views of slaves exist: they can be supremely loyal or utterly corrupt. Homer's insight that slavery diminishes the mind compounds the problem. Some masters brutalize slaves into deeper servility; others take the opposite approach. The root difficulty is that humans resist manageable categorization into slave, free, and master.
distilled: Slavery creates an irresolvable tension: slaves can be supremely loyal yet are diminished by servitude itself, and human nature resists the neat division into slave, free, and master.
notes: Speakers: Megillus (brief), Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (questioning). The quotation from Homer (Odyssey 17.322-323) is deployed with devastating effect: slavery itself corrupts the slave's soul, making the institution self-undermining. The observation that brutal treatment makes souls 'many times more slavish' shows Plato's understanding that oppression degrades rather than disciplines. The statement that 'man is a troublesome animal' -- difficult to manage under the slave/free/master division -- comes remarkably close to questioning slavery's conceptual foundations. Cf. Republic 563b-c on the inversion of master-slave relations in democracy, and Aristotle's attempt to resolve this tension through 'natural slavery' (Politics I.5).

narrative:
"He is a troublesome piece of goods, as has been often shown by the frequent revolts of the Messenians, and the great mischiefs which happen in states having many slaves who speak the same language, and the numerous robberies and lawless life of the Italian banditti, as they are called.

A man who considers all this is fairly at a loss. Two remedies alone remain to us -- not to have the slaves of the same country, nor if possible, speaking the same language; in this way they will more easily be held in subjection: secondly, we should tend them carefully, not only out of regard to them, but yet more out of respect to ourselves.

And the right treatment of slaves is to behave properly to them, and to do to them, if possible, even more justice than to those who are our equals; for he who naturally and genuinely reverences justice, and hates injustice, is discovered in his dealings with any class of men to whom he can easily be unjust. And he who in regard to the natures and actions of his slaves is undefiled by impiety and injustice, will best sow the seeds of virtue in them; and this may be truly said of every master, and tyrant, and of every other having authority in relation to his inferiors.

Slaves ought to be punished as they deserve, and not admonished as if they were freemen, which will only make them conceited. The language used to a servant ought always to be that of a command, and we ought not to jest with them, whether they are males or females -- this is a foolish way which many people have of setting up their slaves, and making the life of servitude more disagreeable both for them and for their masters."

"True."

"Now that each of the citizens is provided, as far as possible, with a sufficient number of suitable slaves who can help him in what he has to do, we may next proceed to describe their dwellings."

"Very good."
refashioned:
"Human beings are difficult property, as the frequent Messenian revolts have proven, along with the chronic troubles in states where many slaves share the same language, and the widespread banditry of the Italian slave populations.

Anyone who considers all this evidence is at a genuine loss. Only two practical remedies exist: first, slaves should not come from the same country or, if possible, speak the same language, making them easier to control. Second, they should be treated with care -- not merely for their sake, but even more for our own.

The right treatment of slaves means behaving justly toward them -- if possible, even more justly than toward our equals. A person who genuinely reveres justice is revealed most clearly in how he treats those he could easily wrong. The master who remains free of impiety and injustice in his treatment of slaves best plants the seeds of virtue in them. This applies to every master, every ruler, and everyone with authority over inferiors.

However, slaves should receive appropriate punishment, not gentle admonishment as though they were free persons -- that only makes them arrogant. Commands, not casual conversation, are proper for servants. Joking with slaves, whether male or female, is a foolish habit that many people fall into, making servitude more unpleasant for both slave and master."

"True."

"Now that each citizen is provided with a sufficient number of suitable slaves to assist with his affairs, we may proceed to discuss their dwellings."

"Very good."
elegant: History proves slavery's dangers: Messenian revolts, same-language slave populations, Italian banditry. Two remedies: diversify slaves' origins and languages for easier control; treat them with justice, even more than equals, since character reveals itself most in treatment of those easily wronged. Just treatment sows virtue in slaves. Yet slaves should receive commands, not friendly banter, and punishment rather than admonishment -- familiarity breeds disorder for both parties.
summary: Slave revolts prove the institution's dangers. Practical remedies include linguistic diversification and just treatment -- since one's character shows most clearly in treatment of the powerless. However, master-slave relations must maintain proper distance: commands rather than conversation, punishment rather than admonishment.
distilled: Slaves should be treated with even greater justice than equals, since character reveals itself in treatment of the powerless, but proper distance must be maintained through commands rather than familiarity.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). This passage contains both Plato's most progressive and most conservative statements on slavery. The principle that justice toward the powerless is the truest test of character is morally profound and potentially subversive of slavery itself. Yet the practical recommendations -- linguistic diversification to prevent solidarity, maintaining social distance -- are designed to preserve the institution. The historical references (Messenian revolts, Italian banditti) ground the discussion in political reality. Cf. Republic 578d-579a on the tyrant's relationship to his slaves as a mirror of soul-disorder. The tension between the moral insight and the institutional conservatism is characteristically Platonic -- the Laws sees the problem without being able to resolve it within its political framework.

narrative:
"The city being new and hitherto uninhabited, care ought to be taken of all the buildings, and the manner of building each of them, and also of the temples and walls. These, Cleinias, were matters which properly came before the marriages; but, as we are only talking, there is no objection to changing the order. If, however, our plan of legislation is ever to take effect, then the house shall precede the marriage if God so will, and afterwards we will come to the regulations about marriage; but at present we are only describing these matters in a general outline."

"Quite true."
refashioned:
"Since the city is new and previously uninhabited, careful attention must be given to all its buildings -- their design and construction methods -- as well as to the temples and walls. These matters, Cleinias, should properly have come before the marriage laws. But since we are only engaged in theoretical discussion, there is no harm in adjusting the order. If, however, our legislative plan ever becomes reality, then God willing, buildings should precede marriages, and marriage regulations will follow. For now, we are describing everything in general outline."

"Quite so."
elegant: Since the city is new and uninhabited, buildings, temples, and walls all require careful planning -- matters that logically precede marriage legislation. The current theoretical discussion allows flexible ordering, but in practice, physical infrastructure must come before domestic arrangements.
summary: The Athenian notes that building design should logically precede marriage laws, since a new city needs physical infrastructure first. The theoretical nature of their discussion permits flexible ordering, but in practice buildings must come before households.
distilled: Building the city's physical infrastructure logically precedes marriage legislation, though theoretical discussion allows flexible ordering.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). This methodological aside is characteristic of the Laws' self-awareness about the difference between theoretical and practical legislation. The acknowledgment that the discussion order does not match the proper order of implementation shows philosophical honesty about the limits of their enterprise. The phrase 'if God so will' (ean theos ethelei) reflects the Laws' pervasive theological humility. Cf. Republic 369c-372d, where the city is built up from physical necessities to higher functions, maintaining a more strictly logical order.

narrative:
"The temples are to be placed all round the agora, and the whole city built on the heights in a circle, for the sake of defence and for the sake of purity. Near the temples are to be placed buildings for the magistrates and the courts of law; in these plaintiff and defendant will receive their due, and the places will be regarded as most holy, partly because they have to do with the holy things: and partly because they are the dwelling-places of holy Gods: and in them will be held the courts in which cases of homicide and other trials of capital offenses may fitly take place.

As to the walls, Megillus, I agree with Sparta in thinking that they should be allowed to sleep in the earth, and that we should not attempt to disinter them; there is a poetical saying, which is finely expressed, that 'walls ought to be of steel and iron, and not of earth; besides, how ridiculous of us to be sending out our young men annually into the country to dig and to trench, and to keep off the enemy by fortifications, under the idea that they are not to be allowed to set foot in our territory, and then, that we should surround ourselves with a wall, which, in the first place, is by no means conducive to the health of cities, and is also apt to produce a certain effeminacy in the minds of the inhabitants, inviting men to run thither instead of repelling their enemies, and leading them to imagine that their safety is due not to their keeping guard day and night, but that when they are protected by walls and gates, then they may sleep in safety; as if they were not meant to labour, and did not know that true repose comes from labour, and that disgraceful indolence and a careless temper of mind is only the renewal of trouble."
refashioned:
"Temples shall be placed around the entire agora, and the whole city shall be built on elevated ground in a circular form, both for defense and for purity. Near the temples shall be buildings for magistrates and courts of law, where plaintiffs and defendants will receive their due. These places shall be considered most sacred, both because they deal with sacred matters and because they house the divine presence. Courts handling homicide and other capital cases shall appropriately be held there.

As for city walls, Megillus, I agree with the Spartan view: let them remain sleeping in the earth; we should not dig them up. There is a fine poetical saying that 'walls should be of steel and iron, not of earth.' Consider the absurdity: we send our young men into the countryside annually to dig trenches and build fortifications to repel the enemy from our territory -- and then we surround ourselves with a wall? City walls are bad for civic health in the first place, and they breed mental softness in the population. Walls invite people to flee behind them instead of confronting their enemies. They encourage the illusion that safety comes from stone barriers and gates rather than from constant vigilance day and night. Walls make people think they can sleep securely without effort -- as if they were not meant to labor, and did not understand that true rest comes only from hard work, and that disgraceful indolence is merely a prelude to greater trouble."
elegant: Temples ring the agora, with magistrates' buildings and courts nearby -- sacred spaces where justice is dispensed in the presence of the gods. But the city shall have no walls. Walls breed effeminacy, encouraging citizens to hide rather than fight, to sleep rather than stand guard. True security comes from vigilance and labor, not stone barriers. Walls of steel and iron -- the courage of citizens -- are the only proper fortification.
summary: The city is designed with temples around the agora and courts nearby as sacred spaces. Agreeing with Sparta, the Athenian rejects city walls: they breed effeminacy, encourage hiding instead of fighting, and create a false sense of security. True safety comes from perpetual vigilance, not physical barriers.
distilled: City walls breed effeminacy and false security -- true walls are the courage and vigilance of citizens, not stone fortifications.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The rejection of city walls is a pro-Spartan position that Plato held consistently (cf. Republic 422d on the needlessness of walls for a virtuous city). The circular hilltop city design combines defensive practicality with symbolic purity. The argument against walls anticipates a key theme of republican political thought: that citizen virtue, not physical fortification, is the true defense. The 'walls of steel and iron' metaphor identifies martial courage as the real fortification. The psychological argument -- that walls produce effeminacy and complacency -- reflects the Laws' broader concern that institutional design shapes character. Cf. Aristotle, Politics VII.11 (1330b-1331a), where he disagrees and advocates walls as practical necessity.

narrative:
"But if men must have walls, the private houses ought to be so arranged from the first that the whole city may be one wall, having all the houses capable of defence by reason of their uniformity and equality towards the streets. The form of the city being that of a single dwelling will have an agreeable aspect, and being easily guarded will be infinitely better for security.

Until the original building is completed, these should be the principal objects of the inhabitants; and the wardens of the city should superintend the work, and should impose a fine on him who is negligent; and in all that relates to the city they should have a care of cleanliness, and not allow a private person to encroach upon any public property either by buildings or excavations.

Further, they ought to take care that the rains from heaven flow off easily, and of any other matters which may have to be administered either within or without the city. The guardians of the law shall pass any further enactments which their experience may show to be necessary, and supply any other points in which the law may be deficient.

And now that these matters, and the buildings about the agora, and the gymnasia, and places of instruction, and theatres, are all ready and waiting for scholars and spectators, let us proceed to the subjects which follow marriage in the order of legislation."

"By all means."
refashioned:
"But if walls are truly necessary, the private houses should be designed from the start so that the entire city forms one continuous wall -- all houses uniform and equal in their orientation toward the streets, creating a defensible perimeter. A city designed as a single integrated dwelling will be both aesthetically pleasing and far easier to guard.

Until the initial construction is complete, these should be the residents' primary concerns. The city wardens shall supervise the work and fine anyone who is negligent. In all city matters, they shall maintain cleanliness and prevent any private citizen from encroaching on public property through unauthorized building or excavation.

They must also ensure proper drainage of rainwater and attend to any other administrative needs inside or outside the city. The guardians of the law shall enact whatever additional legislation experience proves necessary, filling any gaps in the existing code.

With buildings around the agora, gymnasia, schools, and theatres all ready and waiting for their students and audiences, let us move on to the legislative topics that follow marriage."

"Let us do so."
elegant: If walls are needed, private houses should form a continuous defensive perimeter through uniform design -- the city as a single fortified dwelling. City wardens enforce construction standards, maintain cleanliness, and protect public property from private encroachment. With the agora, gymnasia, schools, and theatres complete, the legislative discussion advances to post-marriage topics.
summary: If walls are necessary, houses should be designed to form a continuous defensive perimeter. City wardens enforce building standards, cleanliness, and protection of public property. With civic infrastructure complete, the discussion moves to post-marriage legislation.
distilled: Private houses should form a unified defensive perimeter if walls are needed, and city wardens must enforce building standards and protect public space from private encroachment.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The concept of houses as walls is a compromise: having rejected formal city walls, the Athenian accepts a defensive architecture where private homes serve a public defensive purpose. This merges private and public functions in architecture, paralleling the Laws' broader project of integrating private and public life. The prohibition on private encroachment of public space reflects perennial urban planning concerns. The mention of gymnasia, schools, and theatres as complete and 'waiting' creates a vivid image of the city's institutional infrastructure ready to receive its citizens. Cf. Republic 369d-372d on the city's material foundations.

narrative:
"Assuming that marriages exist already, Cleinias, the mode of life during the year after marriage, before children are born, will follow next in order. In what way bride and bridegroom ought to live in a city which is to be superior to other cities, is a matter not at all easy for us to determine. There have been many difficulties already, but this will be the greatest of them, and the most disagreeable to the many. Still I cannot but say what appears to me to be right and true, Cleinias."

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"Assuming marriages are now established, Cleinias, the next topic in order is how the couple should live during the first year of marriage before children arrive. Determining how bride and bridegroom ought to conduct themselves in a city that aspires to superiority is no easy task. We have encountered many difficulties already, but this will be the greatest -- and the most unpopular with the general public. Nevertheless, I must say what I believe to be right and true."

"You certainly must."
elegant: The first year of marriage, before children, requires legislation -- the most difficult and unpopular topic yet. A city aspiring to excellence cannot leave this period unregulated, however disagreeable the proposal may be to the public.
summary: The Athenian announces that regulating the first year of marriage will be the most difficult and unpopular topic yet, but insists that truth must be spoken regardless of public displeasure.
distilled: Regulating the first year of marriage is the most difficult and unpopular legislative topic, but a city aspiring to excellence cannot avoid it.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The foreshadowing of difficulty and unpopularity builds dramatic tension for the coming proposal about common meals for women (6.58-61). The Athenian's insistence on speaking truth regardless of popularity echoes Socrates' commitment in the Apology (29d-30b) and anticipates the Laws' perennial tension between philosophical truth and political acceptability. The transition from constitutional design to intimate regulation marks Book 6's most controversial territory.

narrative:
"He who imagines that he can give laws for the public conduct of states, while he leaves the private life of citizens wholly to take care of itself; who thinks that individuals may pass the day as they please, and that there is no necessity of order in all things; he, I say, who gives up the control of their private lives, and supposes that they will conform to law in their common and public life, is making a great mistake.

Why have I made this remark? Why, because I am going to enact that the bridegrooms should live at the common tables, just as they did before marriage. This was a singularity when first enacted by the legislator in your parts of the world, Megillus and Cleinias, as I should suppose, on the occasion of some war or other similar danger, which caused the passing of the law, and which would be likely to occur in thinly-peopled places, and in times of pressure. But when men had once tried and been accustomed to a common table, experience showed that the institution greatly conduced to security; and in some such manner the custom of having common tables arose among you."

"Likely enough."
refashioned:
"Anyone who imagines he can legislate for the public conduct of states while leaving citizens' private lives entirely to their own devices -- who thinks individuals can spend their days however they wish and that order is unnecessary in all aspects of life -- is making a profound error. He gives up control over private conduct and naively assumes that people who are undisciplined in private will somehow conform to law in public. That is a great mistake.

Why do I raise this point? Because I am about to propose that bridegrooms continue living at the common tables just as they did before marriage. This institution probably seemed strange when first enacted in your region, Megillus and Cleinias -- likely introduced under the pressure of some war or similar crisis, the kind of emergency common in sparsely populated areas. But once people tried communal dining and grew accustomed to it, experience demonstrated that the practice significantly enhanced security. That is probably how the custom of common tables arose among you."

"That seems likely."
elegant: The great mistake of legislation is regulating public conduct while leaving private life uncontrolled. People undisciplined in private will not obey law in public. Therefore bridegrooms must continue eating at common tables after marriage, an institution that probably originated under wartime necessity but proved its value through experience. Private and public virtue are inseparable.
summary: The Athenian argues that regulating only public life while ignoring private conduct is a fundamental legislative error. He proposes that bridegrooms continue attending common meals after marriage, an institution that originated from wartime necessity but proved its enduring value.
distilled: Legislating public conduct while leaving private life unregulated is a fundamental error -- bridegrooms must continue at common tables because private and public virtue are inseparable.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The principle that private conduct must be regulated alongside public behavior is one of the Laws' most characteristic -- and most controversial -- positions. It marks a dramatic departure from modern liberal thought, which separates public and private spheres. The common meals (syssitia) were a defining feature of Spartan and Cretan society. The historical explanation -- wartime origin, experiential validation -- shows the Laws' pragmatic approach to institutional design. Cf. Republic 416d-417b on communal living for guardians, and Aristotle's criticism of Spartan syssitia (Politics II.10, 1271a). The Laws extends communal dining beyond a warrior elite to married couples, a significant expansion.

narrative:
"I said that there may have been singularity and danger in imposing such a custom at first, but that now there is not the same difficulty. There is, however, another institution which is the natural sequel to this, and would be excellent, if it existed anywhere, but at present it does not. The institution of which I am about to speak is not easily described or executed; and would be like the legislator 'combing wool into the fire,' as people say, or performing any other impossible and useless feat."

"What is the cause, Stranger, of this extreme hesitation?"
refashioned:
"As I was saying, introducing common tables may have been strange and risky at first, but the difficulty is past. There is, however, another institution that naturally follows from this one -- an institution that would be excellent if it existed anywhere, but currently it does not. What I am about to propose is not easily described or carried out. It would be like the legislator 'combing wool into fire,' as the saying goes -- attempting something seemingly impossible and futile."

"What is causing you such extreme hesitation, Stranger?"
elegant: Common tables for men are established precedent. But the natural sequel -- an institution that exists nowhere -- is so radical that proposing it feels like combing wool into fire: an apparently impossible and futile act.
summary: The Athenian acknowledges that common tables are now accepted but hesitates before proposing their natural sequel -- an institution so radical it exists nowhere and would seem impossible to implement.
distilled: The natural sequel to common tables is so radical that proposing it feels like attempting the impossible -- an institution that exists nowhere in the Greek world.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The dramatic buildup -- the Athenian's extreme hesitation, the proverbial 'combing wool into fire' -- creates suspense before revealing the proposal of common tables for women (6.60). The admission that this institution 'does not exist anywhere' underscores its radicalism. The proverb about combing wool into fire describes attempting something both impossible and destructive. The Athenian's hesitation is dramatically authentic -- even the philosophical legislator recognizes the limits of political acceptability. Cf. Republic 452a-e, where Socrates similarly hesitates before proposing female gymnastics.

narrative:
"You shall hear without any fruitless loss of time. That which has law and order in a state is the cause of every good, but that which is disordered or ill-ordered is often the ruin of that which is well-ordered; and at this point the argument is now waiting.

For with you, Cleinias and Megillus, the common tables of men are, as I said, a heaven-born and admirable institution, but you are mistaken in leaving the women unregulated by law. They have no similar institution of public tables in the light of day, and just that part of the human race which is by nature prone to secrecy and stealth on account of their weakness -- I mean the female sex -- has been left without regulation by the legislator, which is a great mistake.

And, in consequence of this neglect, many things have grown lax among you, which might have been far better, if they had been only regulated by law; for the neglect of regulations about women may not only be regarded as a neglect of half the entire matter, but in proportion as woman's nature is inferior to that of men in capacity for virtue, in that degree the consequence of such neglect is more than twice as important."
refashioned:
"Let me tell you without further delay. Whatever has law and order in a state produces every good; whatever is disordered or badly ordered often undermines even what is well-ordered. This is precisely where our argument stands.

Your common tables for men, Cleinias and Megillus, are an admirable, almost divinely inspired institution. But you have made a grave error in leaving women entirely unregulated by law. Women have no comparable institution of public dining. Yet the very part of the human race that is, by nature, more inclined toward secrecy and privacy owing to its relative weakness -- I mean the female sex -- has been left completely outside the legislator's reach. This is a serious mistake.

Because of this neglect, many aspects of your society have become lax that could have been far better managed under proper regulation. Neglecting women's regulation is not merely neglecting half the population. To the degree that women's natural capacity for virtue is less developed than men's, the consequences of neglecting their regulation are more than doubly important."
elegant: The great legislative failure: common tables exist for men but not for women. Leaving the female half of the population unregulated is not merely neglecting half the matter -- since women are more prone to secrecy and less habituated to public virtue, the neglect is more than doubly harmful. Order in a state produces every good; disorder undermines even what is well-ordered.
summary: The Athenian reveals his radical proposal: women need common tables too. Leaving women unregulated is not merely neglecting half the population -- given their lesser habituation to public virtue, the consequences are more than doubly harmful. Order produces every good; disorder undermines even the well-ordered.
distilled: Leaving women without public institutions like common tables neglects more than half the matter, since their lesser habituation to public virtue makes the consequences of neglect disproportionately severe.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage is simultaneously radical and conservative. The proposal for women's common tables extends public institutional life to women -- genuinely progressive for ancient Greece. Yet the justification relies on assumptions about women's 'inferior capacity for virtue' and natural inclination toward 'secrecy and stealth.' The argument that neglecting women is 'more than twice as important' creates a paradox: the less capable sex requires more regulation, making women the more consequential legislative target. Cf. Republic 451c-457b on equal education and roles for women, which is more egalitarian in its premises but also more utopian. The Laws' approach is more patronizing but arguably more politically realistic for its context.

narrative:
"The careful consideration of this matter, and the arranging and ordering on a common principle of all our institutions relating both to men and women, greatly conduces to the happiness of the state. But at present, such is the unfortunate condition of mankind, that no man of sense will even venture to speak of common tables in places and cities in which they have never been established at all; and how can any one avoid being utterly ridiculous, who attempts to compel women to show in public how much they eat and drink?

There is nothing at which the sex is more likely to take offence. For women are accustomed to creep into dark places, and when dragged out into the light they will exert their utmost powers of resistance, and be far too much for the legislator. And therefore, as I said before, in most places they will not endure to have the truth spoken without raising a tremendous outcry, but in this state perhaps they may.

And if we may assume that our whole discussion about the state has not been mere idle talk, I should like to prove to you, if you will consent to listen, that this institution is good and proper; but if you had rather not, I will refrain."
refashioned:
"Carefully considering this matter and organizing all our institutions -- for both men and women -- on a common principle would greatly contribute to the state's happiness. But given the unfortunate state of human affairs, no sensible person would even dare to propose common tables in places where they have never existed. And how can anyone avoid looking utterly ridiculous by trying to compel women to eat and drink under public observation?

Nothing would offend women more. Women are accustomed to operating in privacy, and when dragged into public view they will resist with all their strength -- more than enough to defeat the legislator. In most places, as I said, they will not tolerate hearing this truth without raising a tremendous outcry. But in our proposed state, perhaps they will accept it.

If we can assume that our entire discussion about the state has not been idle theorizing, I would like to demonstrate that this institution is both good and fitting -- if you are willing to listen. If not, I will say no more."
elegant: Extending common tables to women would greatly benefit the state, but the proposal is practically impossible in most places. Women are habituated to privacy and will fiercely resist public regulation of their domestic life. In most cities, even speaking this truth provokes outcry. But in a newly founded city, built on sound principles from the start, the institution might succeed.
summary: Common tables for women would benefit the state but face enormous practical resistance. Women, accustomed to privacy, will fiercely oppose any public regulation of their domestic life. Only in a new city, built from sound principles, might this institution be accepted.
distilled: Women's common tables would greatly benefit the state, but women's habituation to privacy makes the proposal practically impossible except in a city founded on sound principles from scratch.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The Athenian's acknowledgment that women will be 'far too much for the legislator' is remarkable -- it concedes that female resistance to regulation is a genuine political force, not merely an obstacle to be overcome. The passage reveals Plato's awareness that proposals must be politically viable, not merely philosophically correct. The distinction between existing cities (where the proposal is impossible) and a new foundation (where it might work) underscores the Laws' liminal position between ideal theory and practical reform. Cf. Republic 452a-e, where Socrates also anticipates ridicule for proposing equal treatment of women. The offer to 'refrain' if unwanted shows the Athenian's respect for his interlocutors' judgment.

narrative:
"There is nothing which we should both of us like better, Stranger, than to hear what you have to say."

"Very good; and you must not be surprised if I go back a little, for we have plenty of leisure, and there is nothing to prevent us from considering in every point of view the subject of law."

"True."

"Then let us return once more to what we were saying at first. Every man should understand that the human race either had no beginning at all, and will never have an end, but always will be and has been; or that it began an immense while ago."

"Certainly."

"Well, and have there not been constitutions and destructions of states, and all sorts of pursuits both orderly and disorderly, and diverse desires of meats and drinks always, and in all the world, and all sorts of changes of the seasons in which animals may be expected to have undergone innumerable transformations of themselves?"

"No doubt."

"And may we not suppose that vines appeared, which had previously no existence, and also olives, and the gifts of Demeter and her daughter, of which one Triptolemus was the minister, and that, before these existed, animals took to devouring each other as they do still?"

"True."
refashioned:
"There is nothing we would rather hear, Stranger."

"Very well. But do not be surprised if I go back somewhat -- we have ample leisure and nothing prevents us from examining every aspect of law thoroughly."

"True enough."

"Then let us return to our starting point. Everyone should understand that the human race either had no beginning and will never end -- always existing and always having existed -- or that it began an inconceivably long time ago."

"Agreed."

"And throughout this span, have there not been the rise and fall of states, every kind of orderly and disorderly pursuit, diverse appetites for food and drink across the entire world, and all manner of seasonal changes through which animals must have undergone countless transformations?"

"Undoubtedly."

"And may we suppose that cultivated plants like vines and olives eventually appeared where none existed before, along with the gifts of Demeter and her daughter -- grain and agriculture, of which Triptolemus was the minister -- and that before these existed, animals simply devoured one another, as they still do?"

"Yes."
elegant: The human race is either eternal or inconceivably ancient. Throughout this vast span, states have risen and fallen, appetites diversified, seasons shifted, and animals transformed. Agriculture -- vines, olives, grain -- eventually appeared where none existed before. Prior to cultivation, animals simply devoured one another. Human civilization is a late development in an ancient or eternal natural history.
summary: The Athenian steps back to establish the vast scope of human history: either eternal or immeasurably old. States rise and fall, appetites change, and agriculture eventually replaces predation. Human civilization is a late development against an ancient natural backdrop.
distilled: Human civilization, including agriculture and political order, is a late development in an inconceivably ancient or eternal natural history of predation and transformation.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias (responding), Athenian Stranger (primary). This passage introduces a cosmological-anthropological digression justifying the regulation of appetites. The two options for human origins -- eternal existence or immense antiquity -- recall Laws Book 3's treatment of cyclical destruction and renewal (676a-682e). The progression from predation to agriculture to civilization provides a natural-historical framework for understanding human appetites as primal forces requiring regulation. Triptolemus, mythical minister of Demeter, symbolizes the civilizing transition from hunting to farming. Cf. Protagoras 320c-323a (the myth of civilization) and Republic 369b-372d (the city of necessity arising from basic appetites).

narrative:
"Again, the practice of men sacrificing one another still exists among many nations; while, on the other hand, we hear of other human beings who did not even venture to taste the flesh of a cow and had no animal sacrifices, but only cakes and fruits dipped in honey, and similar pure offerings, but no flesh of animals; from these they abstained under the idea that they ought not to eat them, and might not stain the altars of the Gods with blood. For in those days men are said to have lived a sort of Orphic life, having the use of all lifeless things, but abstaining from all living things."

"Such has been the constant tradition, and is very likely true."

"Some one might say to us, 'What is the drift of all this?'"

"A very pertinent question, Stranger."

"And therefore I will endeavour, Cleinias, if I can, to draw the natural inference."

"Proceed."
refashioned:
"Furthermore, the practice of human sacrifice still exists among many peoples. On the other hand, we hear of others who would not even taste beef, offered no animal sacrifices, but brought only cakes, fruits dipped in honey, and similar pure offerings -- no flesh of any kind. They abstained from meat in the belief that eating animals was wrong and that the altars of the gods should not be stained with blood. In those days, people are said to have lived an Orphic life: using everything lifeless but abstaining from all living things."

"That is a well-established tradition, and probably true."

"Someone might well ask us: 'What is the point of all this?'"

"A very fair question, Stranger."

"Then I will try, Cleinias, to draw the natural conclusion from all of this."

"Please do."
elegant: Human history spans the full range of dietary practice: from human sacrifice to pure vegetarian Orphic piety that refused to stain divine altars with blood. The contrast between these extremes reveals something essential about human appetites and their regulation. The natural inference from this vast anthropological survey is about to be drawn.
summary: Human dietary practices range from human sacrifice to strict Orphic vegetarianism. This anthropological contrast between extremes -- consuming human beings versus refusing even animal flesh -- sets up the Athenian's inference about the regulation of fundamental appetites.
distilled: Human history spans from human sacrifice to Orphic vegetarianism, revealing the extreme range of appetites that law must regulate.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The Orphic life -- using lifeless things but abstaining from all living creatures -- represents for Plato a primordial purity that organized religion and custom later corrupted. The range from human sacrifice to Orphic vegetarianism dramatizes the scope of human appetites, setting up the argument (6.64) that three fundamental desires (food, drink, sex) must be mastered by law, reason, and fear. The reference to keeping altars unstained by blood connects dietary regulation to theological purity. Cf. Republic 372a-c on the 'healthy city' with its simple, vegetarian diet, and Laws Book 8 (840b-841e) on regulating sexual appetite.

narrative:
"I see that among men all things depend upon three wants and desires, of which the end is virtue, if they are rightly led by them, or the opposite if wrongly.
