Now these are eating and drinking, which begin at birth -- every animal has a natural desire for them, and is violently excited, and rebels against him who says that he must not satisfy all his pleasures and appetites, and get rid of all the corresponding pains -- and the third and greatest and sharpest want and desire breaks out last, and is the fire of sexual lust, which kindles in men every species of wantonness and madness.

And these three disorders we must endeavour to master by the three great principles of fear and law and right reason; turning them away from that which is called pleasantest to the best, using the Muses and the Gods who preside over contests to extinguish their increase and influx."
refashioned:
"I can see that among human beings, everything depends upon three fundamental wants and desires. If properly guided, they lead to virtue; if misguided, to its opposite. The first two are eating and drinking, which begin at birth -- every animal naturally desires them, becomes violently agitated when denied, and rebels against anyone who says it must not satisfy every appetite and escape every discomfort.

The third is the greatest and sharpest desire, emerging last: the fire of sexual passion, which kindles in people every form of excess and madness.

These three powerful drives must be mastered by three equally powerful principles: fear, law, and right reason. These principles redirect human desire from what seems most pleasant toward what is truly best, employing the arts and the gods of competition to diminish the force and flow of these appetites."
elegant: All human affairs depend on three primal desires: hunger, thirst, and sexual passion -- the fiercest of all. If rightly guided they produce virtue; wrongly guided, vice and madness. Three corresponding principles must master them: fear, law, and right reason. The arts and competitive festivals redirect desire from mere pleasure toward genuine good.
summary: Three fundamental desires -- eating, drinking, and sexual passion -- determine whether human life tends toward virtue or vice. Three corresponding principles -- fear, law, and right reason -- must master these drives, redirecting them from mere pleasure toward what is truly good.
distilled: Three primal desires (hunger, thirst, sex) determine human character, and three corresponding principles (fear, law, reason) must redirect them from pleasure toward virtue.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage provides the psychological foundation for the Laws' entire regulatory program. The three desires correspond roughly to the Republic's three parts of the soul: appetite (hunger/thirst), spirit (partially), and eros (sexual desire). But the Laws' treatment is more practical: instead of philosophical education producing internal harmony (Republic 441d-442b), the Laws relies on external regulators -- fear, law, and reason. The 'Muses and the Gods who preside over contests' connect artistic education and athletic competition to appetite regulation -- culture as a technology of self-governance. Cf. Republic 439d-441c on the tripartite soul, and Phaedrus 246a-254e on the charioteer controlling the horses of the soul.

narrative: "But to return: After marriage let us speak of the birth of children, and after their birth of their nurture and education. In the course of discussion the several laws will be perfected, and we shall at last arrive at the common tables. Whether such associations are to be confined to men, or extended to women also, we shall see better when we approach and take a nearer view of them; and we may then determine what previous institutions are required and will have to precede them. As I said before we shall see them more in detail, and shall be better able to lay down the laws which are proper or suited to them."
refashioned: "But let us return to the main sequence. After marriage comes the birth of children, and after birth, their nurture and education. As our discussion progresses, the various laws will be refined, and we shall eventually reach the topic of common tables. Whether these communal meals should be restricted to men or extended to women as well is a question better addressed when we examine the matter more closely. At that point we can also determine what preliminary institutions need to be established beforehand. As I said, a closer examination will enable us to lay down the most appropriate and well-suited laws."
elegant: The legislative sequence returns to its proper order: marriage, birth, nurture, education, and eventually common tables. Whether common tables extend to women remains an open question for later, closer examination. Good legislation requires approaching each topic in its proper sequence and detail.
summary: The Athenian returns to the legislative sequence: birth, nurture, and education follow marriage. The question of whether common tables should include women is deferred for closer future examination, along with the preliminary institutions required.
distilled: The legislative sequence proceeds from marriage through birth, nurture, and education, with the question of women's common tables deferred for closer examination.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This transitional passage demonstrates the Laws' methodological self-awareness: the discussion of women's common tables (6.59-61) was a digression, and the Athenian now returns to the main legislative sequence. The deferral of the common-tables question to later books (it is partially addressed in Laws Book 7) shows that the Laws treats difficult questions iteratively rather than definitively. The ordering principle -- marriage, birth, nurture, education -- follows the natural lifecycle and mirrors the Republic's educational curriculum (Republic 376c-412a), though the Laws addresses a broader population rather than just guardians.

narrative:
"Very true."

"Let us keep in mind the words which have now been spoken; for hereafter there may be need of them."

"What do you bid us keep in mind?"

"That which we comprehended under the three words -- first, eating, secondly, drinking, thirdly, the excitement of love."

"We shall be sure to remember, Stranger."

"Very good. Then let us now proceed to marriage, and teach persons in what way they shall beget children, threatening them, if they disobey, with the terrors of the law."

"What do you mean?"
refashioned:
"Very true."

"Let us remember what has been said, since we may need it later."

"What should we keep in mind?"

"The three things we identified: first, eating; second, drinking; third, sexual desire."

"We will certainly remember, Stranger."

"Good. Then let us return to marriage and instruct people on how they should bring children into the world, with the threat of legal penalties for those who disobey."

"What do you mean by that?"
elegant: The three fundamental appetites -- eating, drinking, sexual desire -- must be kept in mind as guiding principles. With these established, the discussion returns to marriage and procreation, backed by the force of law against the disobedient.
summary: The Athenian reminds the group to remember the three fundamental appetites -- eating, drinking, and sexual desire -- as they return to marriage legislation, now backed by legal enforcement for procreation.
distilled: The three fundamental appetites must guide marriage legislation, with legal penalties enforcing proper procreation.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias (responding), Athenian Stranger (primary). This brief exchange serves as a structural pivot, connecting the anthropological digression on appetites (6.62-64) back to the practical marriage legislation. The three appetites function as the Laws' psychological framework for all subsequent regulation of intimate life. The mention of 'threatening with the terrors of the law' signals the transition from persuasive preludes (6.44-46) to enforceable statutes (6.67-68). Cf. Republic 571b-572b on the tyrannical soul driven by lawless appetites, which the Laws seeks to prevent through institutional regulation rather than philosophical self-knowledge.

narrative:
"The bride and bridegroom should consider that they are to produce for the state the best and fairest specimens of children which they can. Now all men who are associated in any action always succeed when they attend and give their mind to what they are doing, but when they do not give their mind or have no mind, they fail; wherefore let the bridegroom give his mind to the bride and to the begetting of children, and the bride in like manner give her mind to the bridegroom, and particularly at the time when their children are not yet born.

And let the women whom we have chosen be the overseers of such matters, and let them in whatever number, large or small, and at whatever time the magistrates may command, assemble every day in the temple of Eileithyia during a third part of the day, and being there assembled, let them inform one another of any one whom they see, whether man or woman, of those who are begetting children, disregarding the ordinances given at the time when the nuptial sacrifices and ceremonies were performed.

Let the begetting of children and the supervision of those who are begetting them continue ten years and no longer, during the time when marriage is fruitful. But if any continue without children up to this time, let them take counsel with their kindred and with the women holding the office of overseer and be divorced for their mutual benefit.

If, however, any dispute arises about what is proper and for the interest of either party, they shall choose ten of the guardians of the law and abide by their permission and appointment.

The women who preside over these matters shall enter into the houses of the young, and partly by admonitions and partly by threats make them give over their folly and error: if they persist, let the women go and tell the guardians of the law, and the guardians shall prevent them."
refashioned:
"The bride and bridegroom should consider that they are producing for the state the best and finest children they possibly can. In every joint undertaking, people succeed when they are attentive and focused, but fail when they are careless or mindless. Therefore the bridegroom should devote his full attention to his bride and to the begetting of children, and the bride should devote equal attention to the bridegroom -- especially during the period before their children are born.

The women we have selected as overseers shall supervise these matters. In whatever number and at whatever times the magistrates command, they shall assemble daily in the temple of Eileithyia for a third of the day. There they shall share reports about anyone -- man or woman -- among those who are begetting children who appears to be disregarding the ordinances established during the wedding sacrifices and ceremonies.

The supervised period of procreation shall last ten years and no longer -- the period during which marriage is naturally fertile. If a couple remains childless after this time, they shall consult with their relatives and the women overseers and divorce for their mutual benefit.

If any dispute arises about what is proper or beneficial for either party, they shall select ten of the guardians of the law to decide, and shall abide by their ruling.

The women overseers shall have the authority to enter the homes of young couples and, through a combination of encouragement and warnings, correct any foolish or improper behavior. If the couple persists, the women shall report to the guardians of the law, who shall intervene."
elegant: Marriage is a civic duty: bride and bridegroom must focus on producing the state's best children. Female overseers, meeting daily at Eileithyia's temple, monitor compliance with procreative ordinances for ten years. Childless couples divorce for mutual benefit after this period. The overseers may enter homes to correct behavior through admonition and threat, escalating to the guardians of the law if needed. Reproduction is too important to be left to private discretion.
summary: Couples must focus on producing the best children for the state. Female overseers monitor procreative conduct for ten years, meeting daily at Eileithyia's temple. Childless couples divorce after this period. Overseers may enter homes to correct improper behavior, escalating to guardians of the law if needed.
distilled: Female overseers monitor married couples' procreative conduct for ten years, with authority to enter homes, correct behavior, and mandate divorce for persistent childlessness.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage represents the Laws' most intrusive regulation of private life. Female overseers (a remarkable institution) monitor procreation, enter homes, and report violations -- a level of state intervention in intimate life that exceeds even the Republic's breeding program (Republic 459d-461e), which at least operated through deception rather than surveillance. The temple of Eileithyia (goddess of childbirth) sacralizes the oversight. The ten-year fertility window with mandatory divorce for childlessness treats reproduction as a civic obligation with a statute of limitations. The female overseers represent one of the Laws' few female officeholding positions. Cf. Statesman 310b on the statesman's role in directing marriages.

narrative:
"But if they too cannot prevent them, they shall bring the matter before the people; and let them write up their names and make oath that they cannot reform such and such an one; and let him who is thus written up, if he cannot in a court of law convict those who have inscribed his name, be deprived of the privileges of a citizen in the following respects: let him not go to weddings nor to the thanksgivings after the birth of children; and if he go, let any one who pleases strike him with impunity; and let the same regulations hold about women: let not a woman be allowed to appear abroad, or receive honour, or go to nuptial and birthday festivals, if she in like manner be written up as acting disorderly and cannot obtain a verdict.

And if, when they themselves have done begetting children according to the law, a man or woman have connection with another man or woman who are still begetting children, let the same penalties be inflicted upon them as upon those who are still having a family; and when the time for procreation has passed let the man or woman who refrains in such matters be held in esteem, and let those who do not refrain be held in the contrary of esteem -- that is to say, disesteem.

Now, if the greater part of mankind behave modestly, the enactments of law may be left to slumber; but, if they are disorderly, the enactments having been passed, let them be carried into execution.

To every man the first year is the beginning of life, and the time of birth ought to be written down in the temples of their fathers as the beginning of existence to every child, whether boy or girl. Let every phratria have inscribed on a whited wall the names of the successive archons by whom the years are reckoned. And near to them let the living members of the phratria be inscribed, and when they depart life let them be erased."
refashioned:
"But if even the guardians of the law cannot correct them, they shall bring the matter before the public. The guardians shall post the offender's name and swear under oath that they were unable to reform the person. Anyone thus publicly named, unless they can successfully prosecute those who inscribed their name, shall lose certain citizen privileges: they may not attend weddings or celebrations after the birth of children. If they appear at such events, anyone may strike them without legal consequence.

The same rules apply to women: a woman publicly listed as disorderly, who cannot secure a verdict in her favor, shall not be permitted to appear in public, receive honors, or attend wedding or birthday celebrations.

If anyone who has already completed their own childbearing has sexual relations with someone still in their procreative period, they shall face the same penalties as those still actively begetting children who violate the rules. Once the procreative period has passed, those who exercise restraint shall be held in esteem; those who do not shall be held in disesteem.

If the majority of citizens behave with propriety, these laws may remain dormant. But if there is widespread disorder, they must be fully enforced.

Every person's first year is the beginning of their life, and the date of birth should be recorded in the ancestral temple for every child, boy or girl, as the commencement of their existence. Every phratria shall maintain a whitened wall inscribed with the names of the successive archons who mark the years. Beside these, the names of living members shall be recorded, and when they die, their names shall be erased."
elegant: Those who defy procreative regulations face public shaming and loss of civic privileges -- exclusion from weddings and birth celebrations, with physical punishment for trespassing. Post-procreative adults who interfere with others still begetting face the same penalties. Restraint after one's own period earns esteem; lack of restraint earns disgrace. These laws remain dormant if citizens behave, but activate when disorder arises. Every birth is recorded in ancestral temples, and each phratria maintains a living register on whitened walls, adding names at birth and erasing them at death.
summary: Citizens who violate procreative regulations face public naming, loss of civic privileges, and exclusion from celebrations. Post-procreative interference with others brings the same penalties. These laws lie dormant unless disorder requires their enforcement. Births are recorded in temples, and each phratria maintains a living register on whitened walls.
distilled: Procreative violations bring public shaming and civic exclusion, laws remain dormant unless disorder demands enforcement, and births are permanently recorded in ancestral temples.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The enforcement mechanism is layered: overseers admonish, guardians intervene, and ultimately public shaming strips civic privileges. The 'sleeping law' concept -- enactments that exist but remain dormant unless needed -- is a sophisticated legislative tool, acknowledging that most regulation should be self-enforcing through social norms. The birth registry on whitened walls creates a visible, public record of civic membership, connecting individual existence to the communal phratria. The erasure at death poignantly mirrors the addition at birth. Cf. Republic 460c-461e on the Republic's more covert management of reproduction. The Laws' approach is more transparent but also more invasive in its mechanisms of social control.

narrative: "The limit of marriageable ages for a woman shall be from sixteen to twenty years at the longest -- for a man, from thirty to thirty-five years; and let a woman hold office at forty, and a man at thirty years. Let a man go out to war from twenty to sixty years, and for a woman, if there appear any need to make use of her in military service, let the time of service be after she shall have brought forth children up to fifty years of age; and let regard be had to what is possible and suitable to each."
refashioned: "The marriageable age for women shall be from sixteen to twenty at the latest. For men, from thirty to thirty-five. Women may hold office beginning at forty, men at thirty. Military service for men extends from age twenty to sixty. If circumstances require women's military service, they shall serve after completing childbearing, up to the age of fifty. In all cases, what is possible and suitable for each individual should be the guiding consideration."
elegant: Marriage ages: women sixteen to twenty, men thirty to thirty-five. Office-holding: women from forty, men from thirty. Military service: men from twenty to sixty, women after childbearing until fifty if needed. All provisions should be adapted to what is possible and suitable for each person.
summary: Book 6 concludes with age specifications: women marry at sixteen to twenty, men at thirty to thirty-five. Women may hold office from forty, men from thirty. Men serve militarily from twenty to sixty, women after childbearing until fifty if needed.
distilled: Women marry at 16-20 and may hold office from 40; men marry at 30-35, hold office from 30, and serve militarily from 20-60 -- with women's military service possible after childbearing.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This closing passage provides the concrete age parameters for civic life. The marriage age gap (women 16-20, men 30-35) reflects Greek norms but also the Laws' interest in pairing mature men with younger women for optimal childrearing. Women's eligibility for office at 40 (after childbearing) and potential military service represent significant departures from Greek practice -- the Laws grants women more civic roles than any historical Greek state. The final clause -- 'regard to what is possible and suitable to each' -- introduces a principle of individual accommodation that tempers the rigidity of the age requirements. Cf. Republic 460e-461a on the guardians' reproductive ages, and Republic 451d-452a on women's military training. Book 6 thus ends with the lifecycle fully mapped: from marriage through procreation, office-holding, military service, and implicitly through to old age.
